title: Saint Petersburg

infobox.value: [4th](/wiki/List_of_European_cities_by_population_within_city_limits "List of European cities by population within city limits") in Europe [2nd](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Russia_by_population "List of cities and towns in Russia by population") in Russia

(Петроград) and later **Leningrad** (Ленинград),[[d]](#cite_note-11) is the [second-largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Russia_by_population "List of cities and towns in Russia by population") in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), after [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), the nation's capital. It is situated on the [River Neva](/wiki/River_Neva "River Neva"), at the head of the [Gulf of Finland](/wiki/Gulf_of_Finland "Gulf of Finland") on the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea"). **Saint Petersburg**,[[c]](#cite_note-10) formerly known as **Petrograd** (Петроград) and later **Leningrad** (Ленинград),[[d]](#cite_note-11) is the [second-largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Russia_by_population "List of cities and towns in Russia by population") in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), after [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), the nation's capital. It is situated on the [River Neva](/wiki/River_Neva "River Neva"), at the head of the [Gulf of Finland](/wiki/Gulf_of_Finland "Gulf of Finland") on the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea"). With an area of
1,439 square kilometers (556 square miles), Saint Petersburg is the smallest administrative division of Russia by area. The city had a population of 5,601,911 residents as of 2021,[[4]](#cite_note-2021Census-5) with more than 6.4 million people living in the [metropolitan area](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_metropolitan_area "Saint Petersburg metropolitan area"). Saint Petersburg is the [fourth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_European_cities_by_population_within_city_limits "List of European cities by population within city limits") in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), the [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_around_the_Baltic_Sea "List of cities and towns around the Baltic Sea") on the Baltic Sea, and the world's [northernmost city](/wiki/List_of_northernmost_items#Cities_and_settlements "List of northernmost items") of more than 1 million residents. As the former capital of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), and a [historically strategic port](/wiki/Ports_of_the_Baltic_Sea "Ports of the Baltic Sea"), it is governed as a [federal city](/wiki/Federal_cities_of_Russia "Federal cities of Russia").

The city was founded by Tsar [Peter the Great](/wiki/Peter_the_Great "Peter the Great") on 27 May 1703 on the site of a [captured Swedish fortress](/wiki/Nyenschantz "Nyenschantz"), and was named after the apostle [Saint Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter").[[8]](#cite_note-12) In Russia, Saint Petersburg is historically and culturally associated with the birth of the Russian Empire and Russia's entry into modern history as a European [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power").[[9]](#cite_note-13) It served as a capital of the [Tsardom of Russia](/wiki/Tsardom_of_Russia "Tsardom of Russia"), and the subsequent Russian Empire, from 1712 to 1918 (being replaced by Moscow for a short period between 1728 and 1730).[[10]](#cite_note-14) After the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") in 1917, the [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") moved their government to Moscow.[[11]](#cite_note-McColl-15) The city was renamed Leningrad after Lenin's death in 1924. It was the site of the [siege of Leningrad](/wiki/Siege_of_Leningrad "Siege of Leningrad") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the most lethal siege in history.[[12]](#cite_note-:12-16) In June 1991, only a few months before the [Belovezha Accords](/wiki/Belovezha_Accords "Belovezha Accords") and the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), voters in [a city-wide referendum](/wiki/1991_Leningrad_municipal_election "1991 Leningrad municipal election") supported restoring the city's original name.[[13]](#cite_note-referendum-17)

As Russia's cultural centre,[[14]](#cite_note-18) Saint Petersburg received over 15 million tourists in 2018.[[15]](#cite_note-19)[[16]](#cite_note-20) It is considered an important economic, scientific, and tourism centre of Russia and Europe. In modern times, the city has been nicknamed "the Northern Capital of Russia" and is home to notable [federal government](/wiki/Government_of_Russia "Government of Russia") bodies such as the [Constitutional Court of Russia](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Russia "Constitutional Court of Russia") and the [Heraldic Council of the President of the Russian Federation](/wiki/Heraldic_Council_of_the_President_of_the_Russian_Federation "Heraldic Council of the President of the Russian Federation"). It is also home to the [National Library of Russia](/wiki/National_Library_of_Russia "National Library of Russia") and a planned location for the [Supreme Court of Russia](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Russia "Supreme Court of Russia"), as well as home to the headquarters of the [Russian Navy](/wiki/Russian_Navy "Russian Navy"), and the [Leningrad Military District](/wiki/Leningrad_Military_District "Leningrad Military District") of the [Russian Armed Forces](/wiki/Russian_Armed_Forces "Russian Armed Forces"). The [Historic Centre of Saint Petersburg and Related Groups of Monuments](/wiki/Historic_Centre_of_Saint_Petersburg_and_Related_Groups_of_Monuments "Historic Centre of Saint Petersburg and Related Groups of Monuments") constitute a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). Saint Petersburg is home to the [Hermitage](/wiki/Hermitage_Museum "Hermitage Museum") (one of the largest [art museums](/wiki/Art_museum "Art museum") in the world), and the [Lakhta Center](/wiki/Lakhta_Center "Lakhta Center") (the [tallest skyscraper in Europe](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Europe "List of tallest buildings in Europe")), and was one of the host cities of the [2018 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup") and the [UEFA Euro 2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020").

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Peter_der-Grosse_1838.jpg/250px-Peter_der-Grosse_1838.jpg)](/wiki/File:Peter_der-Grosse_1838.jpg)

While not originally named for Tsar [Peter the Great](/wiki/Peter_the_Great "Peter the Great"), during World War I the city was changed from the Germanic "Petersburg" to "Petrograd" in his honour.

The [name day](/wiki/Name_day "Name day") of Tsar [Peter the Great](/wiki/Peter_the_Great "Peter the Great") falls on 29 June, when the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") observes the memory of [apostles](/wiki/Apostles_in_the_New_Testament "Apostles in the New Testament") [Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter") and [Paul](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle"). The consecration of the small wooden church in their names (its construction began at the same time as the citadel) made them the heavenly patrons of the [Peter and Paul Fortress](/wiki/Peter_and_Paul_Fortress "Peter and Paul Fortress"), while Saint Peter at the same time became the [eponym](/wiki/Eponym "Eponym") of the whole city. When in June 1703 Peter the Great renamed the site after Saint Peter, he did not issue a naming act that established an official spelling; even in his own letters he used diverse spellings, such as Санктьпетерсьбурк (*Sanktpetersburk*), emulating German *Sankt Petersburg*, and Сантпитербурх (*Santpiterburkh*), emulating [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") *Sint-Pietersburgh*, as Peter was multilingual and a Hollandophile. The name was later normalized and [russified](/wiki/Russified "Russified") to Санкт-Петербург (*Sankt-Peterburg*).[[17]](#cite_note-21)[[18]](#cite_note-22)[[19]](#cite_note-Pospelov-23)

A former spelling of the city's name in English was *Saint Petersburgh*. This spelling survives in the name of a [street](/wiki/St._Petersburgh_Place "St. Petersburgh Place") in the [Bayswater](/wiki/Bayswater "Bayswater") district of [London](/wiki/London "London"), near [St Sophia's Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Sophia_Cathedral,_London "Saint Sophia Cathedral, London"), named after a visit by the Tsar to London in 1814.[[20]](#cite_note-24)

A 14 to 15-letter-long name, composed of the three [roots](/wiki/Word_root "Word root"), proved too cumbersome, and many shortened versions were used. The first General Governor of the city [Menshikov](/wiki/Aleksandr_Danilovich_Menshikov "Aleksandr Danilovich Menshikov") is maybe also the author of the first nickname of Petersburg, which he called Петри (*Petri*). It took some years until the known Russian spelling of this name finally settled. In 1740s [Mikhail Lomonosov](/wiki/Mikhail_Lomonosov "Mikhail Lomonosov") uses a derivative of Greek: Πετρόπολις (Петрополис, *Petropolis*) in a Russified form *Petropol'* (Петрополь). A combo *Piterpol* (Питерпол) also appears at this time.[[21]](#cite_note-nester-25) In any case, eventually the usage of the prefix "*Sankt-*" ceased except for formal official documents, where a three-letter abbreviation "СПб" (*SPb*) was very widely used as well.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/St_Petersburg_port_entrance_cropped.jpg/250px-St_Petersburg_port_entrance_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Petersburg_port_entrance_cropped.jpg)

From 1924 to 1991 the city was known as 'Leningrad'. This photograph of the Saint Petersburg port entrance shows an old 'Ленинград' (Leningrad) sign.

In the early 19th century a slavicised variant appeared: *Petrograd* (Russian: Петроград, IPA: [[pʲɪtrɐˈgrat]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Russian "Help:IPA/Russian")),[[e]](#cite_note-orth-26) used also by [Alexander Pushkin](/wiki/Alexander_Pushkin "Alexander Pushkin").[[22]](#cite_note-27) However, it was only on 31 August [[O.S.](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates") 18 August] 1914, after [the war](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") with Germany had begun, that Tsar [Nicholas II](/wiki/Nicholas_II "Nicholas II") renamed the city Petrograd in order to expunge the German words *[Sankt](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Sankt#German "wikt:Sankt")* and *[Burg](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Burg#German "wikt:Burg")*.[[23]](#cite_note-vokrugsveta.ru-28) Since the prefix "Saint" was omitted,[[24]](#cite_note-29) this act also changed the [eponym](/wiki/Eponym "Eponym") and the "patron" of the city from Saint Peter to Peter the Great, its founder.[[19]](#cite_note-Pospelov-23) On 26 January 1924, shortly after the death of [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin"), it was renamed to *Leningrad* (Russian: Ленинград, IPA: [[lʲɪnʲɪnˈgrat]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Russian "Help:IPA/Russian")), meaning 'Lenin City'. On 6 September 1991, the original name, *Sankt-Peterburg*, was returned by a citywide referendum. Today, in English, the city is known as *Saint Petersburg*. Residents often refer to the city by its shortened nickname, *Piter* (Russian: Питер, IPA: [[ˈpʲitʲɪr]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Russian "Help:IPA/Russian")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Admiralty_Embankment_of_Neva_River_in_Saint_Petersburg_during_the_White_nightsIMG_4236.JPG/250px-Admiralty_Embankment_of_Neva_River_in_Saint_Petersburg_during_the_White_nightsIMG_4236.JPG)](/wiki/File:Admiralty_Embankment_of_Neva_River_in_Saint_Petersburg_during_the_White_nightsIMG_4236.JPG)

Embankment of the [Neva](/wiki/Neva "Neva") at 23:11, 22 June 2013

After the October Revolution, the name *Red Petrograd* (Красный Петроград, *Krasny Petrograd*) was often used in newspapers and other prints until the city was renamed *Leningrad* in January 1924.

The referendum on restoring the historic name was held on 12 June 1991, with 55% of voters supporting "*Saint Petersburg*" and 43% supporting "*Leningrad*".[[13]](#cite_note-referendum-17) Renaming the city *Petrograd* was not an option. This change officially took effect on 6 September 1991.[[25]](#cite_note-Orttung-30) Meanwhile, the oblast which surrounds Saint Petersburg is still named [Leningrad](/wiki/Leningrad_Oblast "Leningrad Oblast").

Having passed the role of capital to Petersburg, Moscow never relinquished the title of "capital", being called *pervoprestolnaya* ('first throned') for 200 years. An equivalent name for Petersburg, the "Northern Capital", has re-entered usage today since several federal institutions were recently moved from Moscow to Saint Petersburg. Solemn descriptive names like "the city of three revolutions" and "the cradle of the October Revolution" used in the Soviet era are reminders of the pivotal events in national history that occurred here. *Petropolis* is a translation of a city name to Greek, and is also a kind of descriptive name: [Πέτρ-](/w/index.php?title=%CE%A0%CE%AD%CF%84%CF%81-&action=edit&redlink=1 "Πέτρ- (page does not exist)") [[el](https://el.wikipedia.org/wiki/%CE%A0%CE%AD%CF%84%CF%81%CF%89%CE%BC%CE%B1 "el:Πέτρωμα")] is a Greek root for 'stone', so the "city from stone" emphasizes the material that had been forcibly made obligatory for construction from the first years of the city[[21]](#cite_note-nester-25) (a modern Greek translation is Αγία Πετρούπολη, *Agia Petroupoli*).[[26]](#cite_note-31)

Saint Petersburg has been traditionally called the "Window to Europe" and the "Window to the West" by the Russians.[[27]](#cite_note-32)[[28]](#cite_note-33) The city is the [northernmost metropolis with more than 1 million people](/wiki/List_of_northernmost_items "List of northernmost items") in the world, and is also often described as the "[Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice") of the North" or the "Russian Venice" due to its many water corridors, as the city is built on swamp and water. Furthermore, it has strongly Western European-inspired architecture and [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture"), which is combined with the city's Russian heritage.[[29]](#cite_note-34)[[30]](#cite_note-35)[[31]](#cite_note-36) Another nickname of Saint Petersburg is "The City of the White Nights" because of a natural phenomenon which arises due to the closeness to the [polar region](/wiki/Polar_region "Polar region") and ensures that in summer the night skies of the city do not get completely dark for a month.[[32]](#cite_note-37)[[33]](#cite_note-38) The city is also often called the "Northern [Palmyra](/wiki/Palmyra "Palmyra")", due to its extravagant architecture.[[34]](#cite_note-39)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Saint Petersburg](/wiki/History_of_Saint_Petersburg "History of Saint Petersburg")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Timeline_of_Saint_Petersburg "Timeline of Saint Petersburg").

### Imperial era (1703–1917)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Imperial era (1703–1917)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Kungsboken-karta-sankt-petersburg.jpg/250px-Kungsboken-karta-sankt-petersburg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kungsboken-karta-sankt-petersburg.jpg)

Peter and Paul Fortress, 1722

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Map_spb_1744_high.jpg/250px-Map_spb_1744_high.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_spb_1744_high.jpg)

Saint Petersburg, 1744

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/SPB_Nevsky_Prospekt_from_Police_Bridge_to_City_Duma%2C_photochrome_1896-1897.jpg/250px-SPB_Nevsky_Prospekt_from_Police_Bridge_to_City_Duma%2C_photochrome_1896-1897.jpg)](/wiki/File:SPB_Nevsky_Prospekt_from_Police_Bridge_to_City_Duma,_photochrome_1896-1897.jpg)

Nevsky Prospekt from restaurant Lejeune in the late 19th century

Swedish colonists built [Nyenskans](/wiki/Nyenskans "Nyenskans"), a fortress at the mouth of the [Neva](/wiki/Neva "Neva") River in 1611, which was later called [Ingermanland](/wiki/Ingermanland "Ingermanland"). The small town of Nyen grew up around the fort. Before the 17th century, this area was inhabited by [Finnic](/wiki/Baltic_Finnic_peoples "Baltic Finnic peoples") [Izhorians](/wiki/Izhorians "Izhorians") and [Votians](/wiki/Votians "Votians"). The [Ingrian Finns](/wiki/Ingrian_Finns "Ingrian Finns") moved to the region from the provinces of [Karelia](/wiki/Karelia_(historical_province_of_Finland) "Karelia (historical province of Finland)") and [Savonia](/wiki/Savonia_(historical_province) "Savonia (historical province)") during the Swedish rule. There was also some [Estonian](/wiki/Estonians "Estonians"), [Karelian](/wiki/Karelians "Karelians"), [Russian](/wiki/Russians "Russians") and [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans") population in the area.[[35]](#cite_note-40)[[36]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Bronze_Horseman_02.jpg/250px-Bronze_Horseman_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bronze_Horseman_02.jpg)

The *[Bronze Horseman](/wiki/Bronze_Horseman "Bronze Horseman")*, monument to Peter the Great

At the end of the 17th century, Peter the Great, who was interested in seafaring and maritime affairs, wanted Russia to gain a seaport to trade with the rest of Europe.[[37]](#cite_note-wilson82-42) He needed a better seaport than the country's main one at the time, [Arkhangelsk](/wiki/Arkhangelsk "Arkhangelsk"), which was on the [White Sea](/wiki/White_Sea "White Sea") in the far north and closed to shipping during the winter.

On 12 May [[O.S.](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates") 1 May] 1703, during the [Great Northern War](/wiki/Great_Northern_War "Great Northern War"), [Peter the Great](/wiki/Peter_the_Great "Peter the Great") captured Nyenskans and soon replaced the fortress.[[38]](#cite_note-43) On 27 May [[O.S.](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates") 16 May] 1703,[[39]](#cite_note-hughes66-44) closer to the [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") (5 km (3 mi) inland from the [gulf](/wiki/Gulf_of_Finland "Gulf of Finland")), on [Zayachy (Hare) Island](/wiki/Hare_Island_(Saint_Petersburg) "Hare Island (Saint Petersburg)"), he laid down the [Peter and Paul Fortress](/wiki/Peter_and_Paul_Fortress "Peter and Paul Fortress"), which became the first brick and stone building of the new city.[[40]](#cite_note-spenc-45)

The city was built by [conscripted peasants](/wiki/Serfdom "Serfdom") from all over Russia; in some years several Swedish [prisoners of war](/wiki/Prisoners_of_war "Prisoners of war") were also involved under the supervision of [Alexander Menshikov](/wiki/Alexander_Danilovich_Menshikov "Alexander Danilovich Menshikov").[[41]](#cite_note-46) Tens of thousands of serfs died while building the city.[[42]](#cite_note-47) Later, the city became the centre of the [Saint Petersburg Governorate](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Governorate "Saint Petersburg Governorate"). Peter moved the capital from Moscow to Saint Petersburg in 1712, nine years before the [Treaty of Nystad](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nystad "Treaty of Nystad") of 1721 ended the war. He referred to Saint Petersburg as the capital (or seat of government) as early as 1704.[[37]](#cite_note-wilson82-42)

During its first few years, the city developed around Trinity Square on the right bank of the Neva, near the Peter and Paul Fortress. However, Saint Petersburg soon started to be built out according to a plan. By 1716, the [Swiss Italian](/wiki/Swiss_Italian "Swiss Italian") [Domenico Trezzini](/wiki/Domenico_Trezzini "Domenico Trezzini") had elaborated a project whereby the city centre would be on [Vasilyevsky Island](/wiki/Vasilyevsky_Island "Vasilyevsky Island") and shaped by a rectangular grid of canals. The project was not completed but is evident in the layout of the streets. In 1716, Peter the Great appointed Frenchman [Jean-Baptiste Alexandre Le Blond](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Alexandre_Le_Blond "Jean-Baptiste Alexandre Le Blond") as the [chief architect](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture") of Saint Petersburg.[[43]](#cite_note-48)

The style of [Petrine Baroque](/wiki/Petrine_Baroque "Petrine Baroque"), developed by Trezzini and other architects and exemplified by such buildings as the [Menshikov Palace](/wiki/Menshikov_Palace "Menshikov Palace"), [Kunstkamera](/wiki/Kunstkamera "Kunstkamera"), [Peter and Paul Cathedral](/wiki/Saints_Peter_and_Paul_Cathedral,_Saint_Petersburg "Saints Peter and Paul Cathedral, Saint Petersburg"), [Twelve Collegia](/wiki/Twelve_Collegia "Twelve Collegia"), became prominent in the city architecture of the early 18th century. In 1724, the [Academy of Sciences](/wiki/Russian_Academy_of_Sciences "Russian Academy of Sciences"), [University](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_State_University "Saint Petersburg State University"), and the Academic Gymnasium were established in Saint Petersburg by Peter the Great.

In 1725, Peter died at age fifty-two. His endeavors to modernise Russia had been opposed by the [Russian nobility](/wiki/Russian_nobility "Russian nobility"). There were several attempts on his life and a treason case involving his son.[[44]](#cite_note-49) In 1728, [Peter II of Russia](/wiki/Peter_II_of_Russia "Peter II of Russia") moved his seat back to Moscow. But four years later, in 1732, under Empress [Anna of Russia](/wiki/Anna_of_Russia "Anna of Russia"), Saint Petersburg was again designated as the capital of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"). It remained the seat of the [Romanov dynasty](/wiki/Romanov_dynasty "Romanov dynasty") and the Imperial Court of the [Russian tsars](/wiki/List_of_Russian_monarchs "List of Russian monarchs"), as well as the seat of the Russian government, for another 186 years until the [communist revolution of 1917](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution").

In 1736–1737, the city suffered from catastrophic fires. To rebuild the damaged boroughs, a committee under [Burkhard Christoph von Münnich](/wiki/Burkhard_Christoph_von_M%C3%BCnnich "Burkhard Christoph von Münnich") commissioned a new plan in 1737. The city was divided into five boroughs, and the city centre was moved to the Admiralty borough, on the east bank between the Neva and [Fontanka](/wiki/Fontanka "Fontanka").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/%D0%94%D0%B2%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%86%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%BF%D0%BB%D0%BE%D1%89%D0%B0%D0%B4%D1%8C_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BA%D1%82-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3%D0%B0._%D0%92%D0%B8%D0%B4_%D0%B8%D0%B7_%D0%AD%D1%80%D0%BC%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B6%D0%B0..JPG/250px-%D0%94%D0%B2%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%86%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%BF%D0%BB%D0%BE%D1%89%D0%B0%D0%B4%D1%8C_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BA%D1%82-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3%D0%B0._%D0%92%D0%B8%D0%B4_%D0%B8%D0%B7_%D0%AD%D1%80%D0%BC%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B6%D0%B0..JPG)](/wiki/File:%D0%94%D0%B2%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%86%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%BF%D0%BB%D0%BE%D1%89%D0%B0%D0%B4%D1%8C_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BA%D1%82-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3%D0%B0._%D0%92%D0%B8%D0%B4_%D0%B8%D0%B7_%D0%AD%D1%80%D0%BC%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B0%D0%B6%D0%B0..JPG)

[Palace Square](/wiki/Palace_Square "Palace Square") backed by the [General staff arch and building](/wiki/General_Staff_Building_(Saint_Petersburg) "General Staff Building (Saint Petersburg)"). As the main square of the Russian Empire, it was the setting of many events of historic significance.

It developed along three radial streets, which meet at the [Admiralty building](/wiki/Admiralty,_Saint_Petersburg "Admiralty, Saint Petersburg") and are now known as [Nevsky Prospect](/wiki/Nevsky_Prospect "Nevsky Prospect") (which is considered the main street of the city), [Gorokhovaya Street](/wiki/Gorokhovaya_Street "Gorokhovaya Street"), and [Voznesensky Avenue](/wiki/Voznesensky_Avenue "Voznesensky Avenue"). [Baroque architecture](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") became dominant in the city during the first sixty years, culminating in the Elizabethan Baroque, represented most notably by Italian [Bartolomeo Rastrelli](/wiki/Bartolomeo_Rastrelli "Bartolomeo Rastrelli") with such buildings as the [Winter Palace](/wiki/Winter_Palace "Winter Palace"). In the 1760s, Baroque architecture was succeeded by [neoclassical architecture](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture").

Established in 1762, the Commission of Stone Buildings of Moscow and Saint Petersburg ruled that no structure in the city could be higher than the Winter Palace and prohibited spacing between buildings. During the reign of [Catherine the Great](/wiki/Catherine_the_Great "Catherine the Great") in the 1760s–1780s, the banks of the Neva were lined with [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") embankments.

However, it was not until 1850 that the first permanent bridge across the Neva, [Annunciation Bridge](/wiki/Annunciation_Bridge "Annunciation Bridge"), was allowed to open. Before that, only [pontoon bridges](/wiki/Pontoon_bridge "Pontoon bridge") were allowed. [Obvodny Canal](/wiki/Obvodny_Canal "Obvodny Canal") (dug in 1769–1833) became the southern limit of the city.

The most prominent neoclassical and [Empire-style](/wiki/Empire_style "Empire style") architects in Saint Petersburg included:

* [Jean-Baptiste Vallin de la Mothe](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Vallin_de_la_Mothe "Jean-Baptiste Vallin de la Mothe") ([Imperial Academy of Arts](/wiki/Imperial_Academy_of_Arts "Imperial Academy of Arts"), [Small Hermitage](/wiki/Small_Hermitage "Small Hermitage"), [Gostiny Dvor](/wiki/Great_Gostiny_Dvor "Great Gostiny Dvor"), [New Holland Arch](/wiki/New_Holland_Arch "New Holland Arch"), [Catholic Church of St. Catherine](/wiki/Church_of_St._Catherine_(Saint_Petersburg) "Church of St. Catherine (Saint Petersburg)"))
* [Antonio Rinaldi](/wiki/Antonio_Rinaldi_(architect) "Antonio Rinaldi (architect)") ([Marble Palace](/wiki/Marble_Palace "Marble Palace"))
* [Yury Felten](/wiki/Yury_Felten "Yury Felten") ([Old Hermitage](/wiki/Hermitage_Museum "Hermitage Museum"), [Chesme Church](/wiki/Chesme_Church "Chesme Church"))
* [Giacomo Quarenghi](/wiki/Giacomo_Quarenghi "Giacomo Quarenghi") (Academy of Sciences, [Hermitage Theatre](/wiki/Hermitage_Theatre "Hermitage Theatre"), [Yusupov Palace](/wiki/Moika_Palace "Moika Palace"))
* [Andrey Voronikhin](/wiki/Andrey_Voronikhin "Andrey Voronikhin") ([Mining Institute](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Mining_University "Saint Petersburg Mining University"), [Kazan Cathedral](/wiki/Kazan_Cathedral,_Saint_Petersburg "Kazan Cathedral, Saint Petersburg"))
* [Andreyan Zakharov](/wiki/Andreyan_Zakharov "Andreyan Zakharov") ([Admiralty building](/wiki/Admiralty,_Saint_Petersburg "Admiralty, Saint Petersburg"))
* [Jean-François Thomas de Thomon](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Thomas_de_Thomon "Jean-François Thomas de Thomon") ([Spit of Vasilievsky Island](/wiki/Spit_of_Vasilievsky_Island "Spit of Vasilievsky Island"))
* [Carlo Rossi](/wiki/Carlo_Rossi_(architect) "Carlo Rossi (architect)") ([Yelagin Palace](/wiki/Yelagin_Palace "Yelagin Palace"), [Mikhailovsky Palace](/wiki/Russian_Museum "Russian Museum"), [Alexandrine Theatre](/wiki/Alexandrine_Theatre "Alexandrine Theatre"), [Senate and Synod Buildings](/wiki/Senate_and_Synod_Building "Senate and Synod Building"), [General staff Building](/wiki/General_Staff_Building_(Saint_Petersburg) "General Staff Building (Saint Petersburg)"), design of many streets and squares)
* [Vasily Stasov](/wiki/Vasily_Stasov "Vasily Stasov") ([Moscow Triumphal Gate](/wiki/Moscow_Triumphal_Gate "Moscow Triumphal Gate"), [Trinity Cathedral](/wiki/Trinity_Cathedral,_Saint_Petersburg "Trinity Cathedral, Saint Petersburg"))
* [Auguste de Montferrand](/wiki/Auguste_de_Montferrand "Auguste de Montferrand") ([Saint Isaac's Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Isaac%27s_Cathedral "Saint Isaac's Cathedral"), [Alexander Column](/wiki/Alexander_Column "Alexander Column"))

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Georg_Wilhelm_Timm_-_Dekabristen-Aufstand.jpg/250px-Georg_Wilhelm_Timm_-_Dekabristen-Aufstand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Georg_Wilhelm_Timm_-_Dekabristen-Aufstand.jpg)

[Decembrist revolt](/wiki/Decembrist_revolt "Decembrist revolt") at the [Senate Square](/wiki/Senate_Square_(Saint_Petersburg) "Senate Square (Saint Petersburg)"), 26 December 1825

In 1810, [Alexander I](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Russia "Alexander I of Russia") established the first engineering [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education"), the [Saint Petersburg Main military engineering School](/wiki/Military_Engineering-Technical_University "Military Engineering-Technical University") in Saint Petersburg. Many monuments commemorate the Russian victory over [Napoleonic France](/wiki/Napoleonic_France "Napoleonic France") in the [Patriotic War of 1812](/wiki/Patriotic_War_of_1812 "Patriotic War of 1812"), including the [Alexander Column](/wiki/Alexander_Column "Alexander Column") by Montferrand, erected in 1834, and the [Narva Triumphal Arch](/wiki/Narva_Triumphal_Arch "Narva Triumphal Arch").

In 1825, the suppressed [Decembrist revolt](/wiki/Decembrist_revolt "Decembrist revolt") against [Nicholas I](/wiki/Nicholas_I_of_Russia "Nicholas I of Russia") took place on the [Senate Square](/wiki/Senate_Square_(Saint_Petersburg) "Senate Square (Saint Petersburg)") in the city, a day after Nicholas assumed the throne.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Admiralty_Chart_No_2239_Bay_and_City_of_Petrograd%2C_Published_1854%2C_Large_Corrections_1908.jpg/250px-Admiralty_Chart_No_2239_Bay_and_City_of_Petrograd%2C_Published_1854%2C_Large_Corrections_1908.jpg)](/wiki/File:Admiralty_Chart_No_2239_Bay_and_City_of_Petrograd,_Published_1854,_Large_Corrections_1908.jpg)

Petrograd in 1916, from an Admiralty chart

By the 1840s, neoclassical architecture had given way to various romanticist styles, which dominated until the 1890s, represented by such architects as [Andrei Stackenschneider](/wiki/Andrei_Stackenschneider "Andrei Stackenschneider") ([Mariinsky Palace](/wiki/Mariinsky_Palace "Mariinsky Palace"), [Beloselsky-Belozersky Palace](/wiki/Beloselsky-Belozersky_Palace "Beloselsky-Belozersky Palace"), [Nicholas Palace](/wiki/Nicholas_Palace "Nicholas Palace"), [New Michael Palace](/wiki/Andrei_Stackenschneider "Andrei Stackenschneider")) and [Konstantin Thon](/wiki/Konstantin_Thon "Konstantin Thon") ([Moskovsky railway station](/wiki/Moskovsky_railway_station_(Saint_Petersburg) "Moskovsky railway station (Saint Petersburg)")).

With the [emancipation of the serfs](/wiki/Emancipation_reform_of_1861 "Emancipation reform of 1861") undertaken by [Alexander II](/wiki/Alexander_II_of_Russia "Alexander II of Russia") in 1861 and an [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), the influx of former peasants into the capital increased greatly. Poor boroughs spontaneously developed on the outskirts of the city. Saint Petersburg surpassed Moscow in population and industrial growth; it became one of the largest industrial cities in Europe, with a major naval base (in [Kronstadt](/wiki/Kronstadt "Kronstadt")), the Neva River, and a seaport on the Baltic.

The names of Saints [Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter") and [Paul](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle"), bestowed upon the [original city's citadel](/wiki/Peter_and_Paul_Fortress "Peter and Paul Fortress") and its [cathedral](/wiki/Saints_Peter_and_Paul_Cathedral,_Saint_Petersburg "Saints Peter and Paul Cathedral, Saint Petersburg") (from 1725 – a [burial vault](/wiki/Burial_vault_(tomb) "Burial vault (tomb)") of Russian emperors) coincidentally were the names of the first two assassinated Russian emperors, [Peter III](/wiki/Peter_III_of_Russia "Peter III of Russia") (1762, supposedly killed in a conspiracy led by his wife, [Catherine the Great](/wiki/Catherine_the_Great "Catherine the Great")) and [Paul I](/wiki/Paul_I_of_Russia "Paul I of Russia") (1801, [Nikolay Alexandrovich Zubov](/wiki/Nikolay_Alexandrovich_Zubov "Nikolay Alexandrovich Zubov") and other conspirators who brought to power [Alexander I](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Russia "Alexander I of Russia"), the son of their victim). The third emperor's assassination took place in Saint Petersburg in 1881 when [Alexander II](/wiki/Alexander_II_of_Russia "Alexander II of Russia") was murdered by [terrorists](/wiki/Narodnaya_Volya "Narodnaya Volya") (see the [Church of the Savior on Spilled Blood](/wiki/Church_of_the_Savior_on_Spilled_Blood "Church of the Savior on Spilled Blood")).

The [Revolution of 1905](/wiki/Revolution_of_1905 "Revolution of 1905") began in Saint Petersburg and spread rapidly into the provinces.

On 1 September 1914, after the outbreak of World War I, the Imperial government renamed the city *Petrograd*,[[23]](#cite_note-vokrugsveta.ru-28) meaning "Peter's City", to remove the German words *[Sankt](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Sankt#German "wikt:Sankt")* and *[Burg](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Burg#German "wikt:Burg")*.

### Revolution and Soviet era (1917–1941)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Revolution and Soviet era (1917–1941)")]

In March 1917, during the [February Revolution](/wiki/February_Revolution "February Revolution") Nicholas II abdicated for himself and on behalf of his son, ending the Russian monarchy and over three hundred years of [Romanov](/wiki/Romanov "Romanov") [dynastic rule](/wiki/Dynastic_rule "Dynastic rule").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Desfile-uno-mayo-II--rusia--russianbolshevik00rossuoft.png/250px-Desfile-uno-mayo-II--rusia--russianbolshevik00rossuoft.png)](/wiki/File:Desfile-uno-mayo-II--rusia--russianbolshevik00rossuoft.png)

[Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") celebrating [1 May](/wiki/International_Workers%27_Day "International Workers' Day") near the [Winter Palace](/wiki/Winter_Palace "Winter Palace") half a year after [taking power](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution"), 1918

On 7 November [[O.S.](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates") 25 October] 1917, the Bolsheviks, led by Vladimir Lenin, stormed the [Winter Palace](/wiki/Winter_Palace "Winter Palace") in an event known thereafter as the October Revolution, which led to the end of the social-democratic [provisional government](/wiki/Russian_Provisional_Government "Russian Provisional Government"), the transfer of all political power to the [Soviets](/wiki/Worker%27s_council "Worker's council"), and the rise of the [Communist Party](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union").[[45]](#cite_note-50) After that the city acquired a new descriptive name, "the city of three revolutions",[[46]](#cite_note-russia-travel-51) referring to the three major developments in the political history of Russia of the early 20th century.

In September and October 1917, German troops [invaded](/wiki/Operation_Albion "Operation Albion") the [West Estonian archipelago](/wiki/West_Estonian_archipelago "West Estonian archipelago") and threatened Petrograd with bombardment and invasion. On 12 March 1918, Lenin transferred the government of [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Soviet_Russia "Soviet Russia") to Moscow, to keep it away from the state border. During the [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War"), in mid-1919, [Russian anti-communist forces](/wiki/White_movement "White movement") with the help of [Estonians](/wiki/Estonian_Land_Forces "Estonian Land Forces") attempted to capture the city, but [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") mobilized the army and forced them to retreat to [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Eirik_Sundvor._Leningrad._Savior-on-the-Blood_%281935%29.jpg/250px-Eirik_Sundvor._Leningrad._Savior-on-the-Blood_%281935%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eirik_Sundvor._Leningrad._Savior-on-the-Blood_(1935).jpg)

Leningrad in 1935

On 26 January 1924, five days after Lenin's death, Petrograd was renamed *Leningrad*.[[47]](#cite_note-52) Later many streets and other [toponyms](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/toponym "wikt:toponym") were renamed accordingly, with names in honour of communist figures replacing historic names given centuries before. The city has over 230 places associated with the life and activities of Lenin. Some of them were turned into museums,[[48]](#cite_note-Kann-53) including the [cruiser *Aurora*](/wiki/Russian_cruiser_Aurora "Russian cruiser Aurora")– a symbol of the October Revolution and the oldest ship in the [Russian Navy](/wiki/Russian_Navy "Russian Navy").

In the 1920s and 1930s, the poor outskirts were reconstructed into [regularly planned boroughs](/wiki/Urban_planning_in_Russia "Urban planning in Russia"). [Constructivist architecture](/wiki/Constructivist_architecture "Constructivist architecture") flourished around that time. Housing became a government-provided [amenity](/wiki/Social_utility "Social utility"); many "bourgeois" apartments were so large that numerous families were assigned to what were called "communal" apartments (*[kommunalkas](/wiki/Kommunal_apartment "Kommunal apartment")*). By the 1930s, 68% of the population lived in such housing under very poor conditions. In 1935, a new general plan was outlined, whereby the city should expand to the south. Constructivism was rejected in favour of a more pompous [Stalinist architecture](/wiki/Stalinist_architecture "Stalinist architecture"). Moving the city centre further from the border with Finland, [Stalin](/wiki/Stalin "Stalin") adopted a plan to build a new city hall with a huge adjacent square at the southern end of [Moskovsky Prospekt](/wiki/Moskovsky_Prospekt "Moskovsky Prospekt"), designated as the new main street of Leningrad. After the [Winter (Soviet-Finnish) war](/wiki/Winter_War "Winter War") in 1939–1940, the Soviet–Finnish border moved northwards. Nevsky Prospekt with Palace Square maintained the functions and the role of a city centre.

In December 1931, Leningrad was administratively separated from [Leningrad Oblast](/wiki/Leningrad_Oblast "Leningrad Oblast"). At that time, it included the Leningrad Suburban District, some parts of which were transferred back to Leningrad Oblast in 1936 and turned into [Vsevolozhsky District](/wiki/Vsevolozhsky_District "Vsevolozhsky District"), [Krasnoselsky District](/wiki/Krasnoselsky_District,_Saint_Petersburg "Krasnoselsky District, Saint Petersburg"), Pargolovsky District and Slutsky District (renamed Pavlovsky District in 1944).[[49]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Sennaia-1900.jpg/250px-Sennaia-1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sennaia-1900.jpg)

The [Saviour Church on Sennaya Square](/wiki/Saviour_Church_on_Sennaya_Square "Saviour Church on Sennaya Square") (pre-1917 photo) in Leningrad was one of many notable church buildings destroyed during [The Thaw](/wiki/Khrushchev_Thaw "Khrushchev Thaw").

During the Soviet era, many historic architectural monuments of the previous centuries were destroyed by the new regime for ideological reasons. While that mainly concerned churches and cathedrals, some other buildings were also demolished.[[50]](#cite_note-55)[[51]](#cite_note-56)[[52]](#cite_note-57)

On 1 December 1934, [Sergey Kirov](/wiki/Sergey_Kirov "Sergey Kirov"), the Bolshevik leader of Leningrad, was assassinated under suspicious circumstances, which became the pretext for the [Great Purge](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge").[[53]](#cite_note-58) In Leningrad, approximately 40,000 were executed during Stalin's purges.[[54]](#cite_note-59)

### World War II (1941–1945)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: World War II (1941–1945)")]

Main article: [Siege of Leningrad](/wiki/Siege_of_Leningrad "Siege of Leningrad")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/RIAN_archive_907_Leningradians_queueing_up_for_water.jpg/250px-RIAN_archive_907_Leningradians_queueing_up_for_water.jpg)](/wiki/File:RIAN_archive_907_Leningradians_queueing_up_for_water.jpg)

Citizens of Leningrad during the 872-day [siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Leningrad "Siege of Leningrad"), in which more than one million civilians died, mostly from starvation, [Nevsky Prospect](/wiki/Nevsky_Prospect "Nevsky Prospect") (then known as the *25 October Prospekt*)

During World War II, [German forces](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") besieged Leningrad following the Axis invasion of the Soviet Union in June 1941.[[55]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-60) The siege lasted 872 days, or almost two and a half years,[[55]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-60) from 8 September 1941 to 27 January 1944.[[56]](#cite_note-61)

The [Siege of Leningrad](/wiki/Siege_of_Leningrad "Siege of Leningrad") proved one of the longest, most destructive, and [most lethal sieges](/wiki/Most_lethal_battles_in_world_history#Sieges_and_urban_combat "Most lethal battles in world history") of a major city in [modern history](/wiki/Modern_history "Modern history"). It isolated the city from food supplies except those provided through the [Road of Life](/wiki/Road_of_Life "Road of Life") across [Lake Ladoga](/wiki/Lake_Ladoga "Lake Ladoga"), which could not make it through until the lake froze. More than one million civilians were killed, mainly from starvation. There were incidents of cannibalism, with around 2,000 residents arrested for eating other people.[[57]](#cite_note-62) Many others escaped or were evacuated, so the city became largely depopulated.

On 1 May 1945, [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin"), in his Supreme Commander Order No. 20, named Leningrad, alongside [Stalingrad](/wiki/Stalingrad "Stalingrad"), [Sevastopol](/wiki/Sevastopol "Sevastopol"), and [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa"), [hero cities](/wiki/Hero_City_(Soviet_Union) "Hero City (Soviet Union)") of the war. A law acknowledging the honorary title of "Hero City" passed on 8 May 1965 (the 20th anniversary of the victory in the Great Patriotic War), during the [Brezhnev era](/wiki/Leonid_Brezhnev "Leonid Brezhnev"). The [Presidium of the Supreme Soviet](/wiki/Presidium_of_the_Supreme_Soviet "Presidium of the Supreme Soviet") of the USSR awarded Leningrad as a Hero City the [Order of Lenin](/wiki/Order_of_Lenin "Order of Lenin") and the [Gold Star medal](/wiki/Gold_Star_medal "Gold Star medal") "for the heroic resistance of the city and tenacity of the survivors of the Siege". The [Hero-City Obelisk](/wiki/Hero-City_Obelisk "Hero-City Obelisk") bearing the [Gold Star sign](/wiki/Gold_Star_medal "Gold Star medal") was installed in April 1985.

### Post-war Soviet era (1945–1991)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Post-war Soviet era (1945–1991)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Hammond_Slides_Leningrad_04.jpg/250px-Hammond_Slides_Leningrad_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hammond_Slides_Leningrad_04.jpg)

View of Lermontovski Prospekt, [Egyptian Bridge](/wiki/Egyptian_Bridge "Egyptian Bridge") and the [Fontanka River](/wiki/Fontanka_River "Fontanka River"), 1972

In October 1946, some territories along the northern coast of the [Gulf of Finland](/wiki/Gulf_of_Finland "Gulf of Finland"), which had been annexed into the USSR from Finland in 1940 under the [peace treaty](/wiki/Moscow_Peace_Treaty "Moscow Peace Treaty") following the [Winter War](/wiki/Winter_War "Winter War"), were transferred from Leningrad Oblast to Leningrad and divided into [Sestroretsky District](/wiki/Sestroretsk "Sestroretsk") and [Kurortny District](/wiki/Kurortny_District "Kurortny District"). These included the town of [Terijoki](/wiki/Terijoki "Terijoki") (renamed [Zelenogorsk](/wiki/Zelenogorsk,_Saint_Petersburg "Zelenogorsk, Saint Petersburg") in 1948).[[49]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-54) Leningrad and many of its suburbs were rebuilt over the post-war decades, partially according to pre-war plans. The 1948 general plan for Leningrad featured radial [urban development](/wiki/Urban_development "Urban development") in the north as well as in the south. In 1953, Pavlovsky District in Leningrad Oblast was abolished, and parts of its territory, including Pavlovsk, merged with Leningrad. In 1954, the settlements [Levashovo](/wiki/Levashovo,_Saint_Petersburg "Levashovo, Saint Petersburg"), [Pargolovo](/wiki/Pargolovo "Pargolovo") and [Pesochny](/wiki/Pesochny,_Russia "Pesochny, Russia") merged with Leningrad.[[49]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Leningrad_1991_%284387699303%29.jpg/250px-Leningrad_1991_%284387699303%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leningrad_1991_(4387699303).jpg)

[Griboedov Canal](/wiki/Griboedov_Canal "Griboedov Canal") and the [Church of the Saviour on Blood](/wiki/Church_of_the_Saviour_on_Blood "Church of the Saviour on Blood"), 1991

Leningrad gave its name to the [Leningrad Affair](/wiki/Leningrad_Affair "Leningrad Affair") (1949–1952), a notable event in the postwar political struggle in the [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR"). It was a product of rivalry between Stalin's potential successors where one side was represented by the leaders of the city [Communist Party](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union") organization – the second most significant one in the country after Moscow. The entire elite leadership of Leningrad was destroyed, including the former mayor [Kuznetsov](/wiki/Alexey_Kuznetsov "Alexey Kuznetsov"), the acting mayor Pyotr Sergeevich Popkov, and all their deputies; overall, 23 leaders were sentenced to the death penalty, 181 to prison or exile (rehabilitated in 1954). About 2,000 ranking officials across the USSR were expelled from the party and the Komsomol and removed from leadership positions.[[58]](#cite_note-Zubkova-63)

The [Leningrad Metro](/wiki/Leningrad_Metro "Leningrad Metro") underground [rapid transit system](/wiki/Rapid_transit_system "Rapid transit system"), designed before the war, opened in 1955 with its first eight stations decorated with [marble](/wiki/Marble "Marble") and [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze"). However, after Stalin died in 1953, the perceived ornamental excesses of Stalinist architecture were abandoned. From the 1960s to the 1980s, many new residential boroughs were built on the outskirts; while the [functionalist](/wiki/Functionalism_(architecture) "Functionalism (architecture)") apartment blocks were nearly identical to each other, many families moved there from *kommunalkas* in the city centre to live in separate apartments.

### Contemporary era (1991–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Contemporary era (1991–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Spb_Views_from_Isaac_Cathedral_May2012_09.jpg/250px-Spb_Views_from_Isaac_Cathedral_May2012_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spb_Views_from_Isaac_Cathedral_May2012_09.jpg)

View of the city from the [Saint Isaac's Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Isaac%27s_Cathedral "Saint Isaac's Cathedral")

[On 12 June 1991](/wiki/1991_Leningrad_elections "1991 Leningrad elections"), simultaneously with the [first Russian SFSR presidential elections](/wiki/1991_Russian_presidential_election "1991 Russian presidential election"), the city authorities arranged for the mayoral elections and a referendum on the city's name, when the original name *Saint Petersburg* was restored. 66% of the total count of votes went to [Anatoly Sobchak](/wiki/Anatoly_Sobchak "Anatoly Sobchak"), who became the first directly elected [mayor of the city](/wiki/List_of_heads_of_Saint_Petersburg_government "List of heads of Saint Petersburg government").[[59]](#cite_note-64)

Meanwhile, economic conditions started to deteriorate as the country's people tried to adapt to major changes. For the first time since the 1940s, food [rationing](/wiki/Rationing "Rationing") was introduced, and the city received humanitarian [food aid](/wiki/World_Food_Programme "World Food Programme") from abroad.[[25]](#cite_note-Orttung-30) This dramatic time was depicted in photographic series of Russian photographer [Alexey Titarenko](/wiki/Alexey_Titarenko "Alexey Titarenko").[[60]](#cite_note-65)[[61]](#cite_note-66) Economic conditions began to improve only at the beginning of the 21st century.[[62]](#cite_note-67) In 1995, a northern section of the [Kirovsko-Vyborgskaya Line](/wiki/Kirovsko-Vyborgskaya_Line "Kirovsko-Vyborgskaya Line") of the [Saint Petersburg Metro](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Metro "Saint Petersburg Metro") was cut off by underground flooding, creating a major obstacle to the city development for almost ten years. On 13 June 1996, Saint Petersburg, alongside Leningrad Oblast and [Tver Oblast](/wiki/Tver_Oblast "Tver Oblast"), signed a power-sharing agreement with the federal government, granting it autonomy.[[63]](#cite_note-newsline-68) This agreement was abolished on 4 April 2002.[[64]](#cite_note-demokratiztsiya-69)

In 1996, [Vladimir Yakovlev](/wiki/Vladimir_Anatolyevich_Yakovlev "Vladimir Anatolyevich Yakovlev") defeated [Anatoly Sobchak](/wiki/Anatoly_Sobchak "Anatoly Sobchak") in the elections for the head of the [city administration](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_City_Administration "Saint Petersburg City Administration"). The title of the city head was changed from "mayor" to "governor".[[65]](#cite_note-70) In 2000, Yakovlev won re-election.[[66]](#cite_note-71) His second term expired in 2004; the long-awaited restoration of the broken subway connection was expected to finish by that time. But in 2003, Yakovlev suddenly resigned, leaving the governor's office to [Valentina Matviyenko](/wiki/Valentina_Matviyenko "Valentina Matviyenko").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/4A7A3069_Moika%2C_Saint_Petersburg_%2835468573803%29.jpg/250px-4A7A3069_Moika%2C_Saint_Petersburg_%2835468573803%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:4A7A3069_Moika,_Saint_Petersburg_(35468573803).jpg)

[Moyka River](/wiki/Moyka "Moyka"), flowing through [Central Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Central_Saint_Petersburg "Central Saint Petersburg")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Troitskiy_Bridge_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Troitskiy_Bridge_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Troitskiy_Bridge_-_panoramio.jpg)

[The Trinity Bridge](/wiki/Trinity_Bridge,_Saint_Petersburg "Trinity Bridge, Saint Petersburg") is a landmark of Art Nouveau design.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Saint_Petersburg%2C_Russia_%2847944909056%29.jpg/250px-Saint_Petersburg%2C_Russia_%2847944909056%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Petersburg,_Russia_(47944909056).jpg)

People walking on the main street of Saint Petersburg, [Nevsky Prospekt](/wiki/Nevsky_Prospekt "Nevsky Prospekt")

The law on the election of the City Governor was changed, breaking the tradition of democratic election by universal suffrage that started in 1991. In 2006, the [city legislature](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Saint_Petersburg "Legislative Assembly of Saint Petersburg") re-approved Matviyenko as governor. Residential building had intensified again; [real-estate prices](/wiki/Real_estate_pricing "Real estate pricing") inflated greatly, which caused many new problems for the preservation of the historical part of the city.

Although the central part of the city has a UNESCO designation (there are about 8,000 architectural monuments in Petersburg), the preservation of its historical and architectural environment became controversial.[[67]](#cite_note-72) After 2005, the demolition of older buildings in the historical centre was permitted.[[68]](#cite_note-73) In 2006, [Gazprom](/wiki/Gazprom "Gazprom") announced an ambitious project to build a skyscraper as part of the Gazprom City complex, with its main tower set to soar significantly higher than the city's most famous landmarks. The tower would be located opposite the [Smolny Cathedral](/wiki/Smolny_Cathedral "Smolny Cathedral") on the [Neva river](/wiki/Neva_river "Neva river"), and critics have warned it could disrupt the architectural harmony of the city's landscape.[[69]](#cite_note-74) Urgent protests by citizens and prominent public figures of Russia against this project were not considered by Governor [Valentina Matviyenko](/wiki/Valentina_Matviyenko "Valentina Matviyenko") and the city authorities until December 2010, when after the statement of President [Dmitry Medvedev](/wiki/Dmitry_Medvedev "Dmitry Medvedev"), the city decided to find a more appropriate location for this project. In the same year, the new location for the project was relocated to [Lakhta](/wiki/Lakhta,_Saint_Petersburg "Lakhta, Saint Petersburg"), a historical area northwest of the city centre, and the new project would be named [Lakhta Center](/wiki/Lakhta_Center "Lakhta Center"). Construction was approved by Gazprom and the city administration and commenced in 2012. The 462 m (1,516 ft) high Lakhta Center has become the first [tallest skyscraper in Russia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Russia "List of tallest buildings in Russia") and [Europe](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Europe "List of tallest buildings in Europe") outside of Moscow.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Geography_of_Saint_Petersburg "Geography of Saint Petersburg")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Panorama_of_Saint_Petersburg_from_Palace_Bridge.jpg/1280px-Panorama_of_Saint_Petersburg_from_Palace_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panorama_of_Saint_Petersburg_from_Palace_Bridge.jpg "The Neva River flows through much of the centre of the city. Left – the Spit of Vasilievsky Island, center – River Neva, Peter and Paul Fortress and Trinity Bridge, right – Palace Embankment with the Winter Palace.")

The [Neva River](/wiki/Neva_River "Neva River") flows through much of the centre of the city. Left – the [Spit of Vasilievsky Island](/wiki/Spit_of_Vasilievsky_Island "Spit of Vasilievsky Island"), center – [River Neva](/wiki/River_Neva "River Neva"), [Peter and Paul Fortress](/wiki/Peter_and_Paul_Fortress "Peter and Paul Fortress") and [Trinity Bridge](/wiki/Trinity_Bridge_(Saint_Petersburg) "Trinity Bridge (Saint Petersburg)"), right – [Palace Embankment](/wiki/Palace_Embankment "Palace Embankment") with the [Winter Palace](/wiki/Winter_Palace "Winter Palace").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Saint_Petersburg_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-07-17.jpg/250px-Saint_Petersburg_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-07-17.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Petersburg_by_Sentinel-2,_2020-07-17.jpg)

Satellite image of Saint Petersburg and its suburbs

The area of Saint Petersburg city proper is 605.8 square kilometres (233.9 square miles). The area of the federal subject is 1,439 square kilometres (556 sq mi), which contains Saint Petersburg proper (consisting of eighty-one municipal *[okrugs](/wiki/Okrug "Okrug")*), nine municipal towns ([Kolpino](/wiki/Kolpino "Kolpino"), [Krasnoye Selo](/wiki/Krasnoye_Selo "Krasnoye Selo"), [Kronstadt](/wiki/Kronstadt "Kronstadt"), [Lomonosov](/wiki/Lomonosov,_Russia "Lomonosov, Russia"), [Pavlovsk](/wiki/Pavlovsk,_Saint_Petersburg "Pavlovsk, Saint Petersburg"), [Petergof](/wiki/Petergof "Petergof"), [Pushkin](/wiki/Pushkin_(town) "Pushkin (town)"), [Sestroretsk](/wiki/Sestroretsk "Sestroretsk"), [Zelenogorsk](/wiki/Zelenogorsk,_Saint_Petersburg "Zelenogorsk, Saint Petersburg")), and twenty-one municipal settlements.

Petersburg is in the middle [taiga](/wiki/Taiga "Taiga") lowlands along the shores of the [Neva Bay](/wiki/Neva_Bay "Neva Bay") of the [Gulf of Finland](/wiki/Gulf_of_Finland "Gulf of Finland"), and the islands of the river delta. The largest are [Vasilyevsky Island](/wiki/Vasilyevsky_Island "Vasilyevsky Island") (besides the [artificial island](/wiki/Artificial_island "Artificial island") between Obvodny canal and [Fontanka](/wiki/Fontanka "Fontanka"), and [Kotlin](/wiki/Kotlin_Island "Kotlin Island") in the [Neva Bay](/wiki/Neva_Bay "Neva Bay")), [Petrogradsky](/wiki/Petrogradsky_Island "Petrogradsky Island"), [Dekabristov](/wiki/Dekabristov_Island "Dekabristov Island") and [Krestovsky](/wiki/Krestovsky_Island "Krestovsky Island"). The latter, together with [Yelagin](/wiki/Yelagin_Island "Yelagin Island") and [Kamenny Island](/wiki/Kamenny_Island "Kamenny Island") are covered mostly by parks. The [Karelian Isthmus](/wiki/Karelian_Isthmus "Karelian Isthmus"), North of the city, is a popular [resort area](/wiki/Resort_area "Resort area"). In the south, Saint Petersburg crosses the [Baltic-Ladoga Klint](/wiki/Baltic-Ladoga_Klint "Baltic-Ladoga Klint") and meets the [Izhora Plateau](/wiki/Izhora_Plateau "Izhora Plateau").

The elevation of Saint Petersburg ranges from the sea level to its highest point of 175.9 metres (577 feet) at the Orekhovaya Hill in the [Duderhof Heights](/wiki/Duderhof_Heights "Duderhof Heights") in the south. Part of the city's territory west of [Liteyny Prospekt](/wiki/Liteyny_Prospekt "Liteyny Prospekt") is no higher than 4 m (13 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_sea_level "Above sea level"), and has suffered from numerous floods. [Floods in Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Floods_in_Saint_Petersburg "Floods in Saint Petersburg") are triggered by a long wave in the Baltic Sea, caused by meteorological conditions, winds, and shallowness of the [Neva Bay](/wiki/Neva_Bay "Neva Bay"). The five most disastrous floods occurred in 1824 (4.21 metres or 13 feet 10 inches above sea level, during which over 300 buildings were destroyed[[f]](#cite_note-75)); 1924 (3.8 m, 12 ft 6 in); 1777 (3.21 m, 10 ft 6 in); 1955 (2.93 m, 9 ft 7 in); and 1975 (2.81 m, 9 ft 3 in). To prevent floods, the [Saint Petersburg Dam](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Dam "Saint Petersburg Dam") has been constructed.[[70]](#cite_note-76)

Since the 18th century, the city's terrain has been raised artificially, at some places by more than 4 m (13 ft), making mergers of several islands, and changing the hydrology of the city. Besides the Neva and its tributaries, other important rivers of the federal subject of Saint Petersburg are [Sestra](/wiki/Sestra_River_(Leningrad_Oblast) "Sestra River (Leningrad Oblast)"), [Okhta](/wiki/Okhta "Okhta"), and [Izhora](/wiki/Izhora "Izhora"). The largest lake is Sestroretsky Razliv in the north, followed by [Lakhtinsky Razliv](/wiki/Lakhtinsky_Razliv "Lakhtinsky Razliv"), Suzdal Lakes, and other smaller lakes.

Due to its northerly location at c. 60° N [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), the [day length](/wiki/Day_length "Day length") in Petersburg varies across seasons, ranging from 5 hours 53 minutes to 18 hours 50 minutes. A period from mid-May to end-July during which it doesn't get darker than [nautical twilight](/wiki/Twilight "Twilight") is called *the [white nights](/wiki/Midnight_sun "Midnight sun")*.

Saint Petersburg is about 165 kilometres (103 miles) southeast of the border with Finland, connected to it via Russia's [M10 highway](/wiki/M10_highway_(Russia) "M10 highway (Russia)") ([E18](/wiki/European_route_E18 "European route E18")), along which there is also a connection to the historic city of [Vyborg](/wiki/Vyborg "Vyborg").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

Main article: [Climate of Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Climate_of_Saint_Petersburg "Climate of Saint Petersburg")

Under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), Saint Petersburg is classified as (Köppen:*Dfb*,Trewartha:*Dcbo*), a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate"). The distinct moderating influence of Baltic Sea cyclones results in mild to hot, humid, short summers and long, moderately cold, wet winters. The climate of Saint Petersburg is close to that of [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), although slightly more continental (i.e., colder in winter and warmer in summer) because of its more eastern location, while slightly less continental than that of Moscow.

The average high temperature in July is 23 °C (73 °F), and the average low temperature in February is −8.5 °C (16.7 °F); an extreme temperature of 37.1 °C (98.8 °F) occurred during the [2010 Northern Hemisphere summer heat wave](/wiki/2010_Northern_Hemisphere_summer_heat_wave "2010 Northern Hemisphere summer heat wave"). A winter low of −35.9 °C (−32.6 °F) was recorded in 1883. The average annual temperature is 5.8 °C (42.4 °F). The Neva River within the city limits usually freezes in November–December and a break-up occurs in April. From December to March, there are 118 days on average with snow cover, which reaches an average snow depth of 19 cm (7.5 in) by February.[[71]](#cite_note-pogoda-77) The frost-free period in the city lasts on average for about 135 days. Despite St. Petersburg's northern location, its winters are warmer than [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow#Climate "Moscow")'s due to the [Gulf of Finland](/wiki/Gulf_of_Finland "Gulf of Finland") and some [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream") influence from Scandinavian winds that can bring temperatures slightly above freezing. The city also has a slightly warmer climate than its suburbs due to the [urban heat island effect](/wiki/Urban_heat_island_effect "Urban heat island effect"). It also has a pretty low [diurnal temperature variation](/wiki/Diurnal_temperature_variation "Diurnal temperature variation"), especially during fall and winter. Weather conditions are quite variable all year round.[[72]](#cite_note-78)[[73]](#cite_note-79)

Average annual [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation "Precipitation") varies across the city, averaging 660 millimetres (26 inches) per year and reaching a maximum in late summer. Due to the cool climate, soil moisture is almost always high because of lower [evapotranspiration](/wiki/Evapotranspiration "Evapotranspiration"). [Air humidity](/wiki/Relative_humidity "Relative humidity") is 78% on average, and there are, on average, 165 [overcast](/wiki/Overcast "Overcast") days per year.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [Demographics of Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Demographics_of_Saint_Petersburg "Demographics of Saint Petersburg")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/St_Petersburg_pop_pyramid_2021.svg/250px-St_Petersburg_pop_pyramid_2021.svg.png)](/wiki/File:St_Petersburg_pop_pyramid_2021.svg)

Population pyramid of St. Petersburg in the 2021 Russian Census

Saint Petersburg is the second-largest city in Russia. As of the [2021 Census](/wiki/Russian_Census_(2021) "Russian Census (2021)"),[[4]](#cite_note-2021Census-5) the federal subject's population is 5,601,911 or 3.9% of the total population of Russia; up from 4,879,566 (3.4%) recorded in the [2010 Census](/wiki/Russian_Census_(2010) "Russian Census (2010)"),[[75]](#cite_note-2010Census-81) and up from 5,023,506 recorded in the [1989 Census](/wiki/Soviet_Census_(1989) "Soviet Census (1989)").[[76]](#cite_note-1989Census-82) Over 6.4 million people reside in the [metropolitan area](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_metropolitan_area "Saint Petersburg metropolitan area").

During the 20th century, the city experienced dramatic population changes. From 2.4 million residents in 1916, its population dropped to less than 740,000 by 1920 during the [Russian Revolution of 1917](/wiki/Russian_Revolution_of_1917 "Russian Revolution of 1917") and [Russian Civil War](/wiki/Russian_Civil_War "Russian Civil War"). The minorities of Germans, Poles, Finns, Estonians, and Latvians were almost completely [transferred](/wiki/Population_transfer_in_the_Soviet_Union "Population transfer in the Soviet Union") from Leningrad during the 1930s.[[87]](#cite_note-martin-93) From 1941 to the end of 1943, population dropped from 3 million to less than 600,000, as people died in battles, starved to death or were evacuated during the [Siege of Leningrad](/wiki/Siege_of_Leningrad "Siege of Leningrad"). Some evacuees returned after the siege, but most influx was due to migration from other parts of the Soviet Union. The city absorbed about 3 million people in the 1950s and grew to over 5 million in the 1980s. From 1991 to 2006, the city's population decreased to 4.6 million, while the suburban population increased due to the privatization of land and a massive move to the suburbs. Based on 2010 census results, the population is over 4.8 million.[[88]](#cite_note-Demoscape0163-94)[[89]](#cite_note-95) For the first half of 2007, the birth rate was 9.1 per 1000[[90]](#cite_note-96) and remained lower than the [death rate](/wiki/Death_rate "Death rate") (until 2012[[91]](#cite_note-gks.ru-97)); people over 65 constitute more than twenty percent of the population; and the median age is about 40 years.[[92]](#cite_note-98) Since 2012 the [birth rate](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate") became higher than the [death rate](/wiki/Death_rate "Death rate").[[91]](#cite_note-gks.ru-97) But in 2020, the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Russia "COVID-19 pandemic in Russia") caused a drop in the birth rate, and the city's population decreased to 5,395,000 people.[[93]](#cite_note-99)

According to various opinion polls, more than half of the residents of Saint Petersburg "believe in [God](/wiki/God "God")" (up to 67% according to [VTsIOM](/wiki/VTsIOM "VTsIOM") data for 2002).

Among the believers, the overwhelming majority of the residents of the city are [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") (57.5%), followed by small minority communities of [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") (0.7%), [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants") (0.6%), and [Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics") (0.5%), and [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhists "Buddhists") (0.1%).[[94]](#cite_note-Religion-100)

In total, roughly 59% of the population of the city is [Christian](/wiki/Christian "Christian"), of which over 90% are Orthodox.[[94]](#cite_note-Religion-100) Non-Abrahamic religions and other faiths are represented by only 1.2% of the total population.[[94]](#cite_note-Religion-100)

There are 268 communities of confessions and [religious](/wiki/Religious "Religious") associations in the city: the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") (130 associations), [Pentecostalism](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") (23 associations), the [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") (19 associations), [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") (13 associations), as well as [Old Believers](/wiki/Old_Believers "Old Believers"), Roman Catholic Church, [Armenian Apostolic Church](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church"), [Georgian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Georgian_Orthodox_Church "Georgian Orthodox Church"), [Seventh-day Adventist Church](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church"), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist"), [Muslim](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Baháʼí](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") and others.[[94]](#cite_note-Religion-100)

229 religious buildings in the city are owned or run by religious associations. Among them are architectural monuments of federal significance. The oldest cathedral in the city is the [Peter and Paul Cathedral](/wiki/Saints_Peter_and_Paul_Cathedral,_Saint_Petersburg "Saints Peter and Paul Cathedral, Saint Petersburg"), built between 1712 and 1733, and the largest is the [Kazan Cathedral](/wiki/Kazan_Cathedral,_Saint_Petersburg "Kazan Cathedral, Saint Petersburg"), completed in 1811.

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Government")]

Further information: [Politics of Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Politics_of_Saint_Petersburg "Politics of Saint Petersburg")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/St_Petersburg%2C_Mariinskiy_Palace.jpg/250px-St_Petersburg%2C_Mariinskiy_Palace.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Petersburg,_Mariinskiy_Palace.jpg)

The city assembly meets in the [Mariinsky Palace](/wiki/Mariinsky_Palace "Mariinsky Palace").

Saint Petersburg is a [federal subject](/wiki/Federal_subject "Federal subject") of Russia (a [federal city](/wiki/Federal_cities_of_Russia "Federal cities of Russia")).[[97]](#cite_note-103) The political life of Saint Petersburg is regulated by the [Charter of Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Charter_of_Saint_Petersburg "Charter of Saint Petersburg") adopted by the city legislature in 1998.[[98]](#cite_note-104) The superior executive body is the [Saint Petersburg City Administration](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_City_Administration "Saint Petersburg City Administration"), led by the [city governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Saint_Petersburg "Governor of Saint Petersburg") (mayor before 1996). Saint Petersburg has a [single-chamber](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") legislature, the [Saint Petersburg Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Legislative_Assembly "Saint Petersburg Legislative Assembly"), which is the city's [regional parliament](/wiki/Regional_parliaments_of_Russia "Regional parliaments of Russia").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/RUS-2016-SPB-Smolny_Institute_02.jpg/250px-RUS-2016-SPB-Smolny_Institute_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:RUS-2016-SPB-Smolny_Institute_02.jpg)

The [Smolny Institute](/wiki/Smolny_Institute "Smolny Institute"), seat of the governor

According to the [federal law](/wiki/Law_of_Russia "Law of Russia") passed in 2004, heads of federal subjects, including the governor of Saint Petersburg, were nominated by the [President of Russia](/wiki/President_of_Russia "President of Russia") and approved by local legislatures. Should the legislature disapprove of the nominee, the President could dissolve it. The former governor, [Valentina Matviyenko](/wiki/Valentina_Matviyenko "Valentina Matviyenko"), was approved according to the new system in December 2006. She was the only woman governor in all of Russia until her resignation on 22 August 2011. Matviyenko stood for elections as a member of the Regional Council of Saint Petersburg and won comprehensively with allegations of rigging and ballot stuffing by the opposition. Russian President [Dmitry Medvedev](/wiki/Dmitry_Medvedev "Dmitry Medvedev") has already backed her for the position of [Speaker](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Federation_Council_(Russia) "Chairman of the Federation Council (Russia)") to the [Federation Council](/wiki/Federation_Council "Federation Council"), and her election qualifies her for that job. After her resignation, [Georgy Poltavchenko](/wiki/Georgy_Poltavchenko "Georgy Poltavchenko") was appointed as the new acting governor the same day. In 2012, following passage of a new federal law,[[99]](#cite_note-105) restoring direct elections of heads of federal subjects, the city charter was again amended to provide for direct elections of governor.[[100]](#cite_note-106) On 3 October 2018, Poltavchenko resigned, and [Alexander Beglov](/wiki/Alexander_Beglov "Alexander Beglov") was appointed acting governor.[[101]](#cite_note-beglov-107)

Saint Petersburg is also the unofficial, *de facto* administrative centre of [Leningrad Oblast](/wiki/Leningrad_Oblast "Leningrad Oblast") (a separate federal subject), and of the [Northwestern Federal District](/wiki/Northwestern_Federal_District "Northwestern Federal District").[[102]](#cite_note-108) Saint Petersburg and Leningrad Oblast share several local departments of federal executive agencies and courts, such as court of arbitration, police, [FSB](/wiki/Federal_Security_Service_(Russia) "Federal Security Service (Russia)"), postal service, drug enforcement administration, penitentiary service, federal registration service, and other federal services.

The [Constitutional Court of Russia](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_Russia "Constitutional Court of Russia") moved to Saint Petersburg from Moscow in May 2008. The relocation of the [Supreme Court of Russia](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Russia "Supreme Court of Russia") from Moscow to Saint Petersburg has been planned since 2014.
