## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Economy_of_Saint_Petersburg "Economy of Saint Petersburg")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%B4%D1%83%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BD%D1%8B%D0%B9_%D1%8D%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%B8%D1%87%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%84%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%83%D0%BC_%282022%29_%2841%29.jpg/250px-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%B4%D1%83%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BD%D1%8B%D0%B9_%D1%8D%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%B8%D1%87%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%84%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%83%D0%BC_%282022%29_%2841%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%B6%D0%B4%D1%83%D0%BD%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BD%D1%8B%D0%B9_%D1%8D%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%BC%D0%B8%D1%87%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%84%D0%BE%D1%80%D1%83%D0%BC_(2022)_(41).jpg)

The [Saint Petersburg International Economic Forum](/wiki/St._Petersburg_International_Economic_Forum "St. Petersburg International Economic Forum") is a major Russian investment forum.

Saint Petersburg is a major trade gateway, serving as the financial and industrial centre of Russia, with specializations in oil and gas trade; shipbuilding yards; [aerospace industry](/wiki/Aerospace "Aerospace"); technology, including radio, electronics, software, and computers; machine building, heavy machinery and transport, including tanks and other [military equipment](/wiki/Military_equipment "Military equipment"); mining; [instrument](/wiki/Tool "Tool") manufacture; ferrous and nonferrous [metallurgy](/wiki/Metallurgy "Metallurgy") (production of aluminium alloys); chemicals, [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Drug "Drug"), and [medical equipment](/wiki/Medical_equipment "Medical equipment"); publishing and printing; food and catering; wholesale and retail; textile and [apparel](/wiki/Apparel "Apparel") industries; and many other businesses. It was also home to Lessner, one of Russia's two pioneering automobile manufacturers (along with [Russo-Baltic](/wiki/Russo-Balt "Russo-Balt")); it was founded by machine tool and boilermaker G.A. Lessner in 1904, with designs by Boris Loutsky, and it survived until 1910.[[104]](#cite_note-110)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Admiralty_Shipyard.jpg/250px-Admiralty_Shipyard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Admiralty_Shipyard.jpg)

[Admiralty Shipyard](/wiki/Admiralty_Shipyard "Admiralty Shipyard")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Power_Machines_building_SPB.jpg/250px-Power_Machines_building_SPB.jpg)](/wiki/File:Power_Machines_building_SPB.jpg)

Power Machines plant building on Sverdlovskaya embankment in Saint Petersburg

Ten percent of the world's power [turbines](/wiki/Turbine "Turbine") are made there at the [LMZ](/wiki/LMZ "LMZ"), which has built over two thousand turbines for [power plants](/wiki/Power_plants "Power plants") across the world.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Major local industries are [Admiralty Shipyard](/wiki/Admiralty_Shipyard "Admiralty Shipyard"), [Baltic Shipyard](/wiki/Baltic_Shipyard "Baltic Shipyard"), [LOMO](/wiki/LOMO "LOMO"), [Kirov Plant](/wiki/Kirov_Plant "Kirov Plant"), [Elektrosila](/wiki/Elektrosila "Elektrosila"), [Izhorskiye Zavody](/wiki/Izhorskiye_Zavody "Izhorskiye Zavody"); also registered in Saint Petersburg are [Sovkomflot](/wiki/Sovkomflot "Sovkomflot"), [Petersburg Fuel Company](/wiki/Petersburg_Fuel_Company "Petersburg Fuel Company") and [SIBUR](/wiki/Sibur "Sibur") among other major Russian and international companies.

The [Port of Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Port_of_Saint_Petersburg "Port of Saint Petersburg") has three large cargo [terminals](/wiki/Ports_of_the_Baltic_Sea "Ports of the Baltic Sea"), Bolshoi Port Saint Petersburg, [Kronstadt](/wiki/Kronstadt "Kronstadt"), and [Lomonosov terminal](/wiki/Lomonosov,_Russia "Lomonosov, Russia").[[105]](#cite_note-111) International [cruise liners](/wiki/Cruise_liners "Cruise liners") have been served at the passenger port at Morskoy Vokzal on the south-west of [Vasilyevsky Island](/wiki/Vasilyevsky_Island "Vasilyevsky Island"). In 2008, the first two berths opened at the [New Passenger Port](/wiki/Passenger_Port_of_St._Petersburg "Passenger Port of St. Petersburg") on the west of the island.[[106]](#cite_note-112) The new passenger terminal is part of the city's "Marine Facade" development project[[107]](#cite_note-113) and was due to have seven berths in operation by 2010.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

A complex system of riverports on both banks of the [Neva River](/wiki/Neva_River "Neva River") are interconnected with the system of seaports, thus making Saint Petersburg the main link between the Baltic Sea and the rest of Russia through the [Volga–Baltic Waterway](/wiki/Volga%E2%80%93Baltic_Waterway "Volga–Baltic Waterway").

The [Saint Petersburg Mint](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Mint "Saint Petersburg Mint") (Monetny Dvor), founded in 1724, is one of the largest [mints](/wiki/Mint_(coin) "Mint (coin)") in the world. It mints [Russian coins](/wiki/Russian_ruble "Russian ruble"), medals, and badges. Saint Petersburg is also home to the oldest and largest Russian foundry, Monumentskulptura, which made thousands of sculptures and statues that now grace the public parks of Saint Petersburg and many other cities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Monuments and [bronze statues](/wiki/Bronze_statues "Bronze statues") of the Tsars, as well as other important historic figures and dignitaries, and other world-famous monuments, such as the sculptures by [Peter Clodt von Jürgensburg](/wiki/Peter_Clodt_von_J%C3%BCrgensburg "Peter Clodt von Jürgensburg"), [Paolo Troubetzkoy](/wiki/Paolo_Troubetzkoy "Paolo Troubetzkoy"), [Mark Antokolsky](/wiki/Mark_Antokolsky "Mark Antokolsky"), and others, were made there.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2007, [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota") opened a [Camry](/wiki/Camry "Camry") plant after investing 5 billion rubles (approx. US$200 million) in Shushary,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] one of the southern suburbs of Saint Petersburg. [Opel](/wiki/Opel "Opel"), [Hyundai](/wiki/Hyundai_Motor_Company "Hyundai Motor Company") and Nissan have also signed deals with the [Russian government](/wiki/Politics_of_Russia "Politics of Russia") to build their automotive plants in Saint Petersburg.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The automotive and auto-parts industry is on the rise there during the last decade.[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*]

Saint Petersburg has a large brewery and distillery industry. Known as Russia's "beer capital" due to the supply and quality of local water, its five large breweries account for over 30% of the country's domestic beer production. They include Europe's second-largest brewery [Baltika](/wiki/Baltika "Baltika"), Vena (both operated by BBH), [Heineken Brewery](/wiki/Heineken_International "Heineken International"), [Stepan Razin](/wiki/Stepan_Razin "Stepan Razin") (both by [Heineken](/wiki/Heineken "Heineken")) and [Tinkoff brewery](/wiki/Tinkoff_brewery "Tinkoff brewery") (SUN-[InBev](/wiki/InBev "InBev")).

The city's many local [distilleries](/wiki/Distilleries "Distilleries") produce a broad range of [vodka](/wiki/Vodka "Vodka") brands. The oldest one is [LIVIZ](/w/index.php?title=LIVIZ&action=edit&redlink=1 "LIVIZ (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%9B%D0%B8%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%B7 "ru:Ливиз")] (founded in 1897). Among the youngest is [Russian Standard Vodka](/wiki/Russian_Standard_Vodka "Russian Standard Vodka") introduced in Moscow in 1998, which opened in 2006 a new $60 million distillery in Petersburg (an area of 30,000 m2 (320,000 sq ft), production rate of 22,500 bottles per hour). In 2007, this brand was exported to over 70 countries.[[108]](#cite_note-114)

Saint Petersburg has the second largest [construction industry](/wiki/Construction "Construction") in Russia, including commercial, housing, and road construction.

In 2006, Saint Petersburg's city budget was 180 billion rubles (about US$7 billion at 2006 exchange rates).[[109]](#cite_note-115) The federal subject's [Gross Regional Product](/wiki/Gross_Regional_Product "Gross Regional Product") as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit) was 3.7 trillion [Russian rubles](/wiki/Russian_ruble "Russian ruble") (or around US$70 billion), ranked 2nd in Russia, after Moscow[[110]](#cite_note-116) and per capita of US$13,000, ranked 12th among Russia's federal subjects,[[111]](#cite_note-117) contributed mostly by wholesale and [retail trade](/wiki/Retail_trade "Retail trade") and repair services (24.7%) as well as processing industry (20.9%) and transportation and telecommunications (15.1%).[[112]](#cite_note-118)

Budget revenues of the city in 2009 amounted to 294.3 billion rubles (about US$10.044 billion at 2009 exchange rates), expenses – 336.3 billion rubles (about US$11.477 billion at 2009 exchange rates). The budget deficit amounted to about 42 billion rubles[[113]](#cite_note-119) (about US$1.433 billion at 2009 exchange rates).

In 2015, St. Petersburg was ranked in 4th place economically amongst all federal subjects of the Russian Federation, surpassed only by Moscow, the Tyumen and Moscow Region.[[114]](#cite_note-120)

## Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

Main articles: [Landmarks of Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Landmarks_of_Saint_Petersburg "Landmarks of Saint Petersburg") and [Kronstadt](/wiki/Kronstadt "Kronstadt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Admiralteystvo_on_water%2C_St.Petersburg_RUS.jpg/250px-Admiralteystvo_on_water%2C_St.Petersburg_RUS.jpg)](/wiki/File:Admiralteystvo_on_water,_St.Petersburg_RUS.jpg)

The Admiralty building in St. Petersburg

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Kazan_Cathedral_-_panoramio_%281%29.jpg/250px-Kazan_Cathedral_-_panoramio_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kazan_Cathedral_-_panoramio_(1).jpg)

[Kazan Cathedral](/wiki/Kazan_Cathedral,_Saint_Petersburg "Kazan Cathedral, Saint Petersburg"), an example of [Neoclassical architecture](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Saint_Isaac%27s_Square_SPB_%2801%29.jpg/250px-Saint_Isaac%27s_Square_SPB_%2801%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Isaac%27s_Square_SPB_(01).jpg)

[Saint Isaac's Square](/wiki/Saint_Isaac%27s_Square "Saint Isaac's Square")

The historic architecture of Saint Petersburg's city centre, mostly [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") and [Neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") buildings of the 18th and 19th centuries, has been largely preserved; although a number of buildings were demolished after the Bolsheviks' seizure of power, during the [Siege of Leningrad](/wiki/Siege_of_Leningrad "Siege of Leningrad") and in recent years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The oldest of the remaining buildings is a wooden house built for [Peter I](/wiki/Peter_the_Great "Peter the Great") in 1703 on the shore of the [Neva](/wiki/Neva "Neva") near Trinity Square. Since 1991, the [Historic Centre of Saint Petersburg and Related Groups of Monuments](/wiki/Historic_Centre_of_Saint_Petersburg_and_Related_Groups_of_Monuments "Historic Centre of Saint Petersburg and Related Groups of Monuments") in Saint Petersburg and Leningrad Oblast have been listed by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site.

The ensemble of [Peter and Paul Fortress](/wiki/Peter_and_Paul_Fortress "Peter and Paul Fortress") with the [Peter and Paul Cathedral](/wiki/Saints_Peter_and_Paul_Cathedral,_Saint_Petersburg "Saints Peter and Paul Cathedral, Saint Petersburg") takes a dominant position on [Zayachy Island](/wiki/Zayachy_Island "Zayachy Island") along the right bank of the [Neva River](/wiki/Neva_River "Neva River"). Each noon, a cannon fires a blank shot from the fortress. The [Saint Petersburg Mosque](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Mosque "Saint Petersburg Mosque"), the largest mosque in Europe when opened in 1913, is on the right bank nearby. The [Spit of Vasilievsky Island](/wiki/Spit_of_Vasilievsky_Island "Spit of Vasilievsky Island"), which splits the river into two largest armlets, the [Bolshaya Neva](/wiki/Bolshaya_Neva "Bolshaya Neva") and [Malaya Neva](/wiki/Malaya_Neva "Malaya Neva"), is connected to the northern bank ([Petrogradsky Island](/wiki/Petrogradsky_Island "Petrogradsky Island")) via the [Exchange Bridge](/wiki/Exchange_Bridge "Exchange Bridge") and occupied by the [Old Saint Petersburg Stock Exchange and Rostral Columns](/wiki/Old_Saint_Petersburg_Stock_Exchange_and_Rostral_Columns "Old Saint Petersburg Stock Exchange and Rostral Columns"). The southern coast of [Vasilyevsky Island](/wiki/Vasilyevsky_Island "Vasilyevsky Island") along the Bolshaya Neva features some of the city's oldest buildings, dating from the 18th century, including the [Kunstkamera](/wiki/Kunstkamera "Kunstkamera"), [Twelve Collegia](/wiki/Twelve_Collegia "Twelve Collegia"), [Menshikov Palace](/wiki/Menshikov_Palace "Menshikov Palace"), and [Imperial Academy of Arts](/wiki/Imperial_Academy_of_Arts "Imperial Academy of Arts"). It hosts one of two campuses of [Saint Petersburg State University](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_State_University "Saint Petersburg State University").

On the southern, left bank of the Neva, connected to the spit of Vasilyevsky Island via the [Palace Bridge](/wiki/Palace_Bridge "Palace Bridge"), lie the [Admiralty building](/wiki/Admiralty_building,_Saint_Petersburg "Admiralty building, Saint Petersburg"), the vast [Hermitage Museum](/wiki/Hermitage_Museum "Hermitage Museum") complex stretching along the [Palace Embankment](/wiki/Palace_Embankment "Palace Embankment"), which includes the Baroque [Winter Palace](/wiki/Winter_Palace "Winter Palace"), former official residence of Russian emperors, as well as the neoclassical [Marble Palace](/wiki/Marble_Palace "Marble Palace"). The Winter Palace faces [Palace Square](/wiki/Palace_Square "Palace Square"), the city's main square with the [Alexander Column](/wiki/Alexander_Column "Alexander Column").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/RUS-2016-Aerial-SPB-Peter_and_Paul_Fortress_02.jpg/250px-RUS-2016-Aerial-SPB-Peter_and_Paul_Fortress_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:RUS-2016-Aerial-SPB-Peter_and_Paul_Fortress_02.jpg)

Aerial view of [Peter and Paul Fortress](/wiki/Peter_and_Paul_Fortress "Peter and Paul Fortress")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/RUS-2016-Aerial-SPB-Field_of_Mars.jpg/250px-RUS-2016-Aerial-SPB-Field_of_Mars.jpg)](/wiki/File:RUS-2016-Aerial-SPB-Field_of_Mars.jpg)

The [Field of Mars](/wiki/Field_of_Mars_(Saint_Petersburg) "Field of Mars (Saint Petersburg)")

[Nevsky Prospekt](/wiki/Nevsky_Prospekt "Nevsky Prospekt"), also on the left bank of the [Neva](/wiki/Neva "Neva"), is the city's main avenue. It starts at the Admiralty and runs eastwards next to Palace Square. Nevsky Prospekt crosses the [Moika](/wiki/Moika "Moika") ([Green Bridge](/wiki/Green_Bridge_(Saint_Petersburg) "Green Bridge (Saint Petersburg)")), [Griboyedov Canal](/wiki/Griboyedov_Canal "Griboyedov Canal") ([Kazansky Bridge](/wiki/Kazansky_Bridge "Kazansky Bridge")), [Sadovaya Street](/wiki/Sadovaya_Street "Sadovaya Street"), the [Fontanka](/wiki/Fontanka "Fontanka") ([Anichkov Bridge](/wiki/Anichkov_Bridge "Anichkov Bridge")), meets [Liteyny Prospekt](/wiki/Liteyny_Prospekt "Liteyny Prospekt") and proceeds to [Uprising Square](/wiki/Uprising_Square "Uprising Square") near the [Moskovsky railway station](/wiki/Moskovsky_Rail_Terminal_(Saint_Petersburg) "Moskovsky Rail Terminal (Saint Petersburg)"), where it meets [Ligovsky Prospekt](/wiki/Ligovsky_Prospekt "Ligovsky Prospekt") and turns to the [Alexander Nevsky Lavra](/wiki/Alexander_Nevsky_Lavra "Alexander Nevsky Lavra"). [The Passage](/wiki/Passage_(department_store) "Passage (department store)"), [Catholic Church of St. Catherine](/wiki/Catholic_Church_of_St._Catherine_(Saint_Petersburg) "Catholic Church of St. Catherine (Saint Petersburg)"), [Book House](/wiki/Singer_House "Singer House") (former [Singer Manufacturing Company](/wiki/Singer_Manufacturing_Company "Singer Manufacturing Company") Building in the [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau") style), [Grand Hotel Europe](/wiki/Grand_Hotel_Europe "Grand Hotel Europe"), [Lutheran Church of Saint Peter and Saint Paul](/wiki/Lutheran_Church_of_Saint_Peter_and_Saint_Paul "Lutheran Church of Saint Peter and Saint Paul"), [Great Gostiny Dvor](/wiki/Great_Gostiny_Dvor "Great Gostiny Dvor"), [Russian National Library](/wiki/Russian_National_Library "Russian National Library"), [Alexandrine Theatre](/wiki/Alexandrine_Theatre "Alexandrine Theatre") behind [Mikeshin](/wiki/Mikeshin "Mikeshin")'s statue of [Catherine the Great](/wiki/Catherine_the_Great "Catherine the Great"), [Kazan Cathedral](/wiki/Kazan_Cathedral,_St._Petersburg "Kazan Cathedral, St. Petersburg"), [Stroganov Palace](/wiki/Stroganov_Palace "Stroganov Palace"), [Anichkov Palace](/wiki/Anichkov_Palace "Anichkov Palace") and [Beloselsky-Belozersky Palace](/wiki/Beloselsky-Belozersky_Palace "Beloselsky-Belozersky Palace") are all along that avenue.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Nevsky_Avenue_01.jpg/250px-Nevsky_Avenue_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nevsky_Avenue_01.jpg)

[Nevsky Prospekt](/wiki/Nevsky_Prospekt "Nevsky Prospekt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Saint_Petersburg_2019.jpg/250px-Saint_Petersburg_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Petersburg_2019.jpg)

[Palace Square](/wiki/Palace_Square "Palace Square") during Christmas

The [Alexander Nevsky Lavra](/wiki/Alexander_Nevsky_Lavra "Alexander Nevsky Lavra"), intended to house the relics of [St. Alexander Nevsky](/wiki/St._Alexander_Nevsky "St. Alexander Nevsky"), is an important centre of [Christian education](/wiki/Religious_studies "Religious studies") in Russia. It also contains the [Tikhvin Cemetery](/wiki/Tikhvin_Cemetery "Tikhvin Cemetery") with graves of many notable Petersburgers.

On the territory between the Neva and Nevsky Prospekt, the [Church of the Savior on Blood](/wiki/Church_of_the_Savior_on_Blood "Church of the Savior on Blood"), Mikhailovsky Palace housing the [Russian Museum](/wiki/Russian_Museum "Russian Museum"), [Field of Mars](/wiki/Field_of_Mars_(Saint_Petersburg) "Field of Mars (Saint Petersburg)"), [St. Michael's Castle](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Castle "St. Michael's Castle"), [Summer Garden](/wiki/Summer_Garden "Summer Garden"), [Tauride Palace](/wiki/Tauride_Palace "Tauride Palace"), [Smolny Institute](/wiki/Smolny_Institute "Smolny Institute") and [Smolny Convent](/wiki/Smolny_Convent "Smolny Convent") are located.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/The_Church_of_the_Saviour_on_Spilled_Blood_%2820956466968%29.jpg/250px-The_Church_of_the_Saviour_on_Spilled_Blood_%2820956466968%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Church_of_the_Saviour_on_Spilled_Blood_(20956466968).jpg)

[Church of the Savior on Blood](/wiki/Church_of_the_Savior_on_Blood "Church of the Savior on Blood"), seen from [Griboyedov Canal](/wiki/Griboyedov_Canal "Griboyedov Canal")

 [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Smolny_Cathedral_SPB_02.jpg/250px-Smolny_Cathedral_SPB_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Smolny_Cathedral_SPB_02.jpg)

[Smolny Convent](/wiki/Smolny_Convent "Smolny Convent"), an example of [Baroque architecture](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture")

Many notable landmarks are to the west and south of the Admiralty Building, including the [Trinity Cathedral](/wiki/Trinity_Cathedral,_Saint_Petersburg "Trinity Cathedral, Saint Petersburg"), [Mariinsky Palace](/wiki/Mariinsky_Palace "Mariinsky Palace"), [Hotel Astoria](/wiki/Hotel_Astoria_(Saint_Petersburg) "Hotel Astoria (Saint Petersburg)"), famous [Mariinsky Theatre](/wiki/Mariinsky_Theatre "Mariinsky Theatre"), [New Holland Island](/wiki/New_Holland_Island "New Holland Island"), [Saint Isaac's Cathedral](/wiki/Saint_Isaac%27s_Cathedral "Saint Isaac's Cathedral"), the largest in the city, and [Senate Square](/wiki/Senate_Square_(Saint_Petersburg) "Senate Square (Saint Petersburg)"), with the [Bronze Horseman](/wiki/Bronze_Horseman "Bronze Horseman"), 18th-century equestrian monument to [Peter the Great](/wiki/Peter_the_Great "Peter the Great"), which is considered among the city's most recognisable symbols. Other symbols of Saint Petersburg include the [weather vane](/wiki/Weather_vane "Weather vane") in the shape of a small ship on top of the Admiralty's golden spire and the golden angel on top of the Peter and Paul Cathedral. The Palace Bridge [drawn](/wiki/Drawbridge "Drawbridge") at night is yet another symbol of the city.

From April to November, 22 bridges across the Neva and main canals are drawn to let ships pass in and out of the Baltic Sea according to a schedule.[[115]](#cite_note-121) It was not until 2004 that the first high bridge across the Neva, which does not need to be drawn, [Big Obukhovsky Bridge](/wiki/Big_Obukhovsky_Bridge "Big Obukhovsky Bridge"), was opened. The most remarkable bridges of our days are Korabelny and Petrovsky cable-stayed bridges, which form the most spectacular part of the city toll road, [Western High-Speed Diameter](/wiki/Western_High-Speed_Diameter "Western High-Speed Diameter"). There are hundreds of smaller [bridges in Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Bridges_in_Saint_Petersburg "Bridges in Saint Petersburg") spanning numerous canals and distributaries of the Neva, some of the most important of which are the [Moika](/wiki/Moika "Moika"), [Fontanka](/wiki/Fontanka "Fontanka"), [Griboyedov Canal](/wiki/Griboyedov_Canal "Griboyedov Canal"), [Obvodny Canal](/wiki/Obvodny_Canal "Obvodny Canal"), [Karpovka](/wiki/Karpovka "Karpovka"), and [Smolenka](/wiki/Smolenka_River "Smolenka River"). Due to the intricate web of canals, Saint Petersburg is often called *[Venice of the North](/wiki/Venice_of_the_North "Venice of the North")*. The rivers and canals in the city centre are lined with granite embankments. The embankments and bridges are separated from rivers and canals by [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") or [cast iron](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron") [parapets](/wiki/Parapet "Parapet").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/RUS-2016-Aerial-SPB-Peterhof_Palace.jpg/250px-RUS-2016-Aerial-SPB-Peterhof_Palace.jpg)](/wiki/File:RUS-2016-Aerial-SPB-Peterhof_Palace.jpg)

Aerial view of [Peterhof Palace](/wiki/Peterhof_Palace "Peterhof Palace")

Southern suburbs of the city feature former imperial residences, including [Petergof](/wiki/Petergof "Petergof"), with majestic fountain cascades and parks, [Tsarskoe Selo](/wiki/Tsarskoe_Selo "Tsarskoe Selo"), with the baroque [Catherine Palace](/wiki/Catherine_Palace "Catherine Palace") and the neoclassical [Alexander Palace](/wiki/Alexander_Palace "Alexander Palace"), and [Pavlovsk](/wiki/Pavlovsk_Palace "Pavlovsk Palace"), which has a domed palace of [Emperor Paul](/wiki/Emperor_Paul "Emperor Paul") and one of Europe's largest English-style parks. Some other residences nearby and making part of the World Heritage site, including a castle and park in [Gatchina](/wiki/Gatchina "Gatchina"), actually belong to [Leningrad Oblast](/wiki/Leningrad_Oblast "Leningrad Oblast") rather than Saint Petersburg. Another notable suburb is [Kronstadt](/wiki/Kronstadt "Kronstadt") with its 19th-century fortifications and naval monuments, occupying the [Kotlin Island](/wiki/Kotlin_Island "Kotlin Island") in the Gulf of Finland.

Since around the end of the 20th century, a great deal of active building and restoration works have been carried out in a number of the city's older districts. The authorities have recently been compelled to transfer the ownership of state-owned private residences in the city centre to private lessors. Many older buildings have been reconstructed to allow their use as apartments and penthouses.

Some of these structures, such as the [Saint Petersburg Commodity and Stock Exchange](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Commodity_and_Stock_Exchange "Saint Petersburg Commodity and Stock Exchange") have been recognised as town-planning errors.[[116]](#cite_note-122)

### Parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Parks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Temple_of_Friendship_in_Pavlovsk_Park_01.jpg/250px-Temple_of_Friendship_in_Pavlovsk_Park_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Temple_of_Friendship_in_Pavlovsk_Park_01.jpg)

The "Temple of Friendship" in [Pavlovsk Park](/wiki/Pavlovsk_Park "Pavlovsk Park")

Saint Petersburg is home to many parks and gardens. Some of the most well-known are in the southern suburbs, including [Pavlovsk](/wiki/Pavlovsk_Palace "Pavlovsk Palace"), one of Europe's largest [English gardens](/wiki/English_garden "English garden"). [Sosnovka](/wiki/Sosnovka "Sosnovka") is the largest park within the city limits, occupying 240 ha. The [Summer Garden](/wiki/Summer_Garden "Summer Garden") is the oldest, dating back to the early 18th century and designed in the regular style. It is on the Neva's southern bank at the head of the Fontanka and is famous for its cast iron railing and marble sculptures.

Among other notable parks are the [Maritime Victory Park](/wiki/Maritime_Victory_Park "Maritime Victory Park") on [Krestovsky Island](/wiki/Krestovsky_Island "Krestovsky Island") and the Moscow Victory Park in the south, both commemorating the victory over [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") in the Second World War, as well as the Central Park of Culture and Leisure occupying [Yelagin Island](/wiki/Yelagin_Island "Yelagin Island") and the [Tauride Garden](/wiki/Tauride_Garden "Tauride Garden") around the [Tauride Palace](/wiki/Tauride_Palace "Tauride Palace"). The most common trees grown in the parks are the [English oak](/wiki/English_oak "English oak"), [Norway maple](/wiki/Norway_maple "Norway maple"), [green ash](/wiki/Green_ash "Green ash"), [silver birch](/wiki/Silver_birch "Silver birch"), [Siberian Larch](/wiki/Siberian_Larch "Siberian Larch"), [blue spruce](/wiki/Blue_spruce "Blue spruce"), [crack willow](/wiki/Salix_euxina "Salix euxina"), [limes](/wiki/Tilia "Tilia"), and [poplars](/wiki/Poplars "Poplars"). Important [dendrological collections](/wiki/Xylotheque "Xylotheque") dating back to the 19th century are hosted by the [Saint Petersburg Botanical Garden](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Botanical_Garden "Saint Petersburg Botanical Garden") and the Park of the Forestry Academy.

In order to commemorate 300 years anniversary of Saint Petersburg, a new park was laid out. The park is in the northwestern part of the city. The construction started in 1995. It is planned to connect the park with the pedestrian bridge to the territory of [Lakhta Center](/wiki/Lakhta_Center "Lakhta Center")'s recreation areas. In the park 300 trees of valuable sorts, 300 decorative apple trees, and 70 limes. 300 other trees and bushes were planted. These trees were presented to Saint Petersburg by non-commercial and educational organizations of the city, its sister-cities, the city of Helsinki, heads of other regions of Russia, German Savings Bank and other people and organizations.[[117]](#cite_note-123)

### Tall structures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Tall structures")]

Regulations forbid the construction of tall buildings in Saint Petersburg's city centre. Until the early 2010s, three [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscrapers "Skyscrapers") were built: Leader Tower (140 m), Alexander Nevsky (124 m), and Atlantic City (105 m) – all situated far from the historical centre. The 310-metre (1,020 ft) tall [Saint Petersburg TV Tower](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_TV_Tower "Saint Petersburg TV Tower"), constructed in 1962, was the tallest structure in the city.

However, a controversial project endorsed by the city authorities was announced, known as the [Okhta Center](/wiki/Okhta_Center "Okhta Center"), to build a 396-metre (1,299 ft) [supertall](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_and_structures_in_the_world "List of tallest buildings and structures in the world") skyscraper. In 2008, the [World Monuments Fund](/wiki/World_Monuments_Fund "World Monuments Fund") included the Saint Petersburg historic skyline on the watch list of the 100 most endangered sites due to the expected construction, which threatened to alter it drastically.[[118]](#cite_note-124) The Okhta Center project was cancelled at the end of 2010.

In 2012, the [Lakhta Center](/wiki/Lakhta_Center "Lakhta Center") project began in the city's outskirts, to include a 463-metre (1,519 ft) tall office skyscraper and several low-rise mixed-use buildings. The latter project caused much less controversy. Unlike the previous unbuilt project, it was not seen by UNESCO as a potential threat to the city's cultural heritage due to its remote location from the historic centre. The skyscraper was completed in 2019, and at 462.5 meters, it is currently the [tallest in Russia and Europe](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Europe "List of tallest buildings in Europe").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/%D0%9B%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B0_%D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D1%80_2021.jpg/250px-%D0%9B%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B0_%D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D1%80_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9B%D0%B0%D1%85%D1%82%D0%B0_%D1%86%D0%B5%D0%BD%D1%82%D1%80_2021.jpg)

[Lakhta Center](/wiki/Lakhta_Center "Lakhta Center"), the tallest building in Europe

Saint Petersburg has a significant historical and cultural heritage.[[119]](#cite_note-125)[[120]](#cite_note-126)[[121]](#cite_note-127)[[122]](#cite_note-128)[[123]](#cite_note-129)[[124]](#cite_note-130)[[125]](#cite_note-131)

The city's 18th and 19th-century architectural ensemble and its environs are preserved in virtually unchanged form. For various reasons (including large-scale destruction during World War II and construction of modern buildings during the postwar period in the largest historical centres of Europe), Saint Petersburg has become a unique reserve of European architectural styles of the past three centuries. Saint Petersburg's loss of capital city status helped it retain many of its pre-revolutionary buildings, as modern architectural 'prestige projects' tended to be built in Moscow; this largely prevented the rise of mid-to-late-20th-century architecture and helped maintain the architectural appearance of the historic city centre.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Andrey_Zeest_-_Amber_Room_2_%28autochrome%29.jpg/250px-Andrey_Zeest_-_Amber_Room_2_%28autochrome%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrey_Zeest_-_Amber_Room_2_(autochrome).jpg)

The [Amber Room](/wiki/Amber_Room "Amber Room") in the [Catherine Palace](/wiki/Catherine_Palace "Catherine Palace")

Saint Petersburg is inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list as an area with 36 historical architectural complexes and around 4000 outstanding individual monuments of architecture, history, and culture. New tourist programs and sightseeing tours have been developed for those wishing to see Saint Petersburg's cultural heritage.

The city has 221 museums, 2,000 libraries, more than 80 theatres, 100 concert organizations, 45 galleries and exhibition halls, 62 cinemas, and 80 other cultural establishments. Every year, the city hosts around 100 festivals and various competitions of art and culture, including more than 50 international ones.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Schloss_Peterhof%2C_Gro%C3%9Fe_Kaskade.jpg/250px-Schloss_Peterhof%2C_Gro%C3%9Fe_Kaskade.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schloss_Peterhof,_Gro%C3%9Fe_Kaskade.jpg)

Grand Peterhof Palace and the Grand Cascade

Despite the [economic instability of the 1990s](/wiki/Economic_history_of_the_Russian_Federation "Economic history of the Russian Federation"), not a single major theatre or museum was closed in Saint Petersburg; on the contrary many new ones opened, for example a private museum of puppets (opened in 1999) is the third museum of its kind in Russia, where collections of more than 2000 dolls are presented including 'The multinational Saint Petersburg' and Pushkin's Petersburg. The museum world of Saint Petersburg is incredibly diverse. The city is not only home to the world-famous [Hermitage Museum](/wiki/Hermitage_Museum "Hermitage Museum") and the [Russian Museum](/wiki/Russian_Museum "Russian Museum") with its rich collection of [Russian art](/wiki/Russian_art "Russian art"), but also the palaces of Saint Petersburg and its suburbs, so-called small-town museums and others like the museum of famous Russian writer [Dostoyevsky](/wiki/Dostoyevsky "Dostoyevsky"); Museum of Musical Instruments, the museum of decorative arts and the museum of professional orientation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Saint_Petersburg_Philharmonia_-_Bolshoi_Zal.jpg/250px-Saint_Petersburg_Philharmonia_-_Bolshoi_Zal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Petersburg_Philharmonia_-_Bolshoi_Zal.jpg)

The Bolshoi Zal (Grand Hall) of [Saint Petersburg Philharmonia](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Philharmonia "Saint Petersburg Philharmonia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/%D0%A1%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%92%D0%B0%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%B5%D0%B2%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%87%D1%8C%D1%8E.jpg/250px-%D0%A1%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%92%D0%B0%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%B5%D0%B2%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%87%D1%8C%D1%8E.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A1%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%BB%D0%BA%D0%B0_%D0%92%D0%B0%D1%81%D0%B8%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%B5%D0%B2%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B3%D0%BE_%D0%BE%D1%81%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%87%D1%8C%D1%8E.jpg)

[Old Saint Petersburg Stock Exchange and Rostral Columns](/wiki/Old_Saint_Petersburg_Stock_Exchange_and_Rostral_Columns "Old Saint Petersburg Stock Exchange and Rostral Columns")

The [musical life](/wiki/Music_of_Russia "Music of Russia") of Saint Petersburg is rich and diverse, with the city now playing host to a number of annual carnivals. Ballet performances occupy a special place in the cultural life of Saint Petersburg. The Petersburg School of Ballet is named as one of the best in the world. Traditions of the Russian classical school have been passed down from generation to generation among outstanding educators. The art of famous and prominent Saint Petersburg dancers like [Rudolf Nureyev](/wiki/Rudolf_Nureyev "Rudolf Nureyev"), [Natalia Makarova](/wiki/Natalia_Makarova "Natalia Makarova"), [Mikhail Baryshnikov](/wiki/Mikhail_Baryshnikov "Mikhail Baryshnikov") was, and is, admired throughout the world. Contemporary Petersburg ballet is made up not only of traditional Russian classical schools but also ballets by those like [Boris Eifman](/wiki/Boris_Eifman "Boris Eifman"), who expanded the scope of strict classical [Russian ballet](/wiki/Russian_ballet "Russian ballet") to almost unimaginable limits. Remaining faithful to the classical basis (he was a choreographer at the [Vaganova Academy of Russian Ballet](/wiki/Vaganova_Academy_of_Russian_Ballet "Vaganova Academy of Russian Ballet")), he combined [classical ballet](/wiki/Classical_ballet "Classical ballet") with the [avant-garde style](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde"), and then, in turn, with acrobatics, rhythmic gymnastics, dramatic expressiveness, [cinema](/wiki/Cinema_of_Russia "Cinema of Russia"), color, light, and finally with spoken word.

## Media and communications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Media and communications")]

All major Russian newspapers are active in Saint Petersburg. The city has a developed telecommunications system. In 2014, [Rostelecom](/wiki/Rostelecom "Rostelecom"), the national operator, announced the beginning of a major modernization of the fixed-line network in the city.[[126]](#cite_note-132)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Society and culture in Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Society_and_culture_in_Saint_Petersburg "Society and culture in Saint Petersburg")

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Museums")]

Further information: [List of museums in Saint Petersburg](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Saint_Petersburg "List of museums in Saint Petersburg")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Hermitage_Museum_in_Saint_Petersburg.jpg/960px-Hermitage_Museum_in_Saint_Petersburg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hermitage_Museum_in_Saint_Petersburg.jpg "The State Hermitage Museum (Hermitage Theatre, Old Hermitage, Small Hermitage and Winter Palace, all part of the current museum complex)")

The [State Hermitage Museum](/wiki/State_Hermitage_Museum "State Hermitage Museum") ([Hermitage Theatre](/wiki/Hermitage_Theatre "Hermitage Theatre"), Old Hermitage, Small Hermitage and [Winter Palace](/wiki/Winter_Palace "Winter Palace"), all part of the current museum complex)

Saint Petersburg is home to more than two hundred museums, many of them in historic buildings. The largest is the [Hermitage Museum](/wiki/Hermitage_Museum "Hermitage Museum") that features the interiors of the former imperial residence and a vast collection of art. The [Russian Museum](/wiki/Russian_Museum "Russian Museum") is a large museum devoted to Russian fine art. The apartments of some famous people, including [Alexander Pushkin](/wiki/Alexander_Pushkin "Alexander Pushkin"), [Fyodor Dostoyevsky](/wiki/Fyodor_Dostoyevsky "Fyodor Dostoyevsky"), [Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov](/wiki/Nikolai_Rimsky-Korsakov "Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov"), [Feodor Chaliapin](/wiki/Feodor_Chaliapin "Feodor Chaliapin"), [Alexander Blok](/wiki/Alexander_Blok "Alexander Blok"), [Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov"), [Anna Akhmatova](/wiki/Anna_Akhmatova "Anna Akhmatova"), [Mikhail Zoshchenko](/wiki/Mikhail_Zoshchenko "Mikhail Zoshchenko"), [Joseph Brodsky](/wiki/Joseph_Brodsky "Joseph Brodsky"), as well as some palace and park ensembles of the southern suburbs and notable architectural monuments such as St. Isaac's Cathedral, have also been turned into public museums.

The [Kunstkamera](/wiki/Kunstkamera "Kunstkamera"), with its collection established in 1714 by Peter the Great to collect curiosities from all over the world, is sometimes considered the first museum in Russia, which has evolved into the present-day [Peter the Great Museum of Anthropology and Ethnography](/wiki/Peter_the_Great_Museum_of_Anthropology_and_Ethnography "Peter the Great Museum of Anthropology and Ethnography"). The [Russian Ethnography Museum](/wiki/Russian_Ethnography_Museum "Russian Ethnography Museum"), which has been split from the Russian Museum, is devoted to the cultures of the people of Russia, the [former Soviet Union](/wiki/Former_Soviet_Union "Former Soviet Union"), and the Russian Empire.

* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Exterior_of_Hermitage_Museum-2.jpg/250px-Exterior_of_Hermitage_Museum-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Exterior_of_Hermitage_Museum-2.jpg "The State Hermitage Museum is the largest art museum in the world by gallery space.[127]")

  The State Hermitage Museum is the [largest art museum](/wiki/List_of_largest_art_museums "List of largest art museums") in the world by [gallery](/wiki/Art_gallery "Art gallery") space.[[127]](#cite_note-133)
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Russian_museam.JPG/250px-Russian_museam.JPG)](/wiki/File:Russian_museam.JPG "The State Russian Museum is the world's largest depository of Russian fine art.")

  The State Russian Museum is the world's largest depository of Russian fine art.
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/%D0%AD%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B3%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%84%D0%B8%D1%87%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%BC%D1%83%D0%B7%D0%B5%D0%B9_%28%D0%A1%D0%9F%D0%91%29.jpg/250px-%D0%AD%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B3%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%84%D0%B8%D1%87%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%BC%D1%83%D0%B7%D0%B5%D0%B9_%28%D0%A1%D0%9F%D0%91%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%AD%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B3%D1%80%D0%B0%D1%84%D0%B8%D1%87%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%BC%D1%83%D0%B7%D0%B5%D0%B9_(%D0%A1%D0%9F%D0%91).jpg "The Russian Museum of Ethnography is one of the largest ethnographic museums in the world.[128]")

  The Russian Museum of Ethnography is one of the largest ethnographic museums in the world.[[128]](#cite_note-134)

Several museums provide insight into the Soviet history of Saint Petersburg, including the Museum of the Blockade, which describes the [Siege of Leningrad](/wiki/Siege_of_Leningrad "Siege of Leningrad") and the Museum of Political History, which explains many authoritarian features of the USSR.

Other notable museums include the [Central Naval Museum](/wiki/Central_Naval_Museum "Central Naval Museum"), and [Zoological Museum](/wiki/Zoological_Museum_of_the_Zoological_Institute_of_the_Russian_Academy_of_Sciences "Zoological Museum of the Zoological Institute of the Russian Academy of Sciences"), [Central Soil Museum](/wiki/The_Central_Soil_Museum_by_V._V._Dokuchaev "The Central Soil Museum by V. V. Dokuchaev"), the [Russian Railway Museum](/wiki/Russian_Railway_Museum "Russian Railway Museum"), [Suvorov Museum](/wiki/Suvorov_Museum "Suvorov Museum"), Museum of the Siege of Leningrad, [Erarta Museum of Contemporary Art](/wiki/Erarta "Erarta"), the largest non-governmental museum of contemporary art in Russia, [Saint Petersburg Museum of History](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Museum_of_History "Saint Petersburg Museum of History") in the [Peter and Paul Fortress](/wiki/Peter_and_Paul_Fortress "Peter and Paul Fortress") and [Artillery Museum](/wiki/Military_Historical_Museum_of_Artillery,_Engineers_and_Signal_Corps "Military Historical Museum of Artillery, Engineers and Signal Corps"), which includes not only artillery items, but also a huge collection of other military equipment, uniforms, and decorations. Amongst others, Saint Petersburg also hosts the State Museum of the History of Religion, one of the oldest museums in Russia about religion, depicting cultural representations from various parts of the globe.[[129]](#cite_note-135)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Profusion_of_gold_at_the_Mariinsky_Theatre_%2818271919528%29.jpg/960px-Profusion_of_gold_at_the_Mariinsky_Theatre_%2818271919528%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Profusion_of_gold_at_the_Mariinsky_Theatre_(18271919528).jpg)

The main auditorium of the Mariinsky Theatre

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/2022-05-18_-_Panorama_of_Stalls_and_Boxes_at_the_Main_Mariinsky_Theatre.jpg/250px-2022-05-18_-_Panorama_of_Stalls_and_Boxes_at_the_Main_Mariinsky_Theatre.jpg)](/wiki/File:2022-05-18_-_Panorama_of_Stalls_and_Boxes_at_the_Main_Mariinsky_Theatre.jpg)

Panorama of stalls and boxes at the Main Mariinsky Theatre

Among the city's more than fifty theatres is the [Mariinsky Theatre](/wiki/Mariinsky_Theatre "Mariinsky Theatre") (formerly known as the Kirov Theatre), home to the [Mariinsky Ballet](/wiki/Mariinsky_Ballet "Mariinsky Ballet") company and opera. Leading ballet dancers, such as [Vaslav Nijinsky](/wiki/Vaslav_Nijinsky "Vaslav Nijinsky"), [Anna Pavlova](/wiki/Anna_Pavlova "Anna Pavlova"), [Rudolf Nureyev](/wiki/Rudolf_Nureyev "Rudolf Nureyev"), [Mikhail Baryshnikov](/wiki/Mikhail_Baryshnikov "Mikhail Baryshnikov"), [Galina Ulanova](/wiki/Galina_Ulanova "Galina Ulanova") and [Natalia Makarova](/wiki/Natalia_Makarova "Natalia Makarova"), were principal stars of the Mariinsky ballet.

The first music school, the [Saint Petersburg Conservatory](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Conservatory "Saint Petersburg Conservatory"), was founded in 1862 by the Russian pianist and composer [Anton Rubinstein](/wiki/Anton_Rubinstein "Anton Rubinstein"). The school alumni have included such notable composers as [Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky](/wiki/Pyotr_Ilyich_Tchaikovsky "Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky"), [Sergei Prokofiev](/wiki/Sergei_Prokofiev "Sergei Prokofiev"), [Artur Kapp](/wiki/Artur_Kapp "Artur Kapp"), [Rudolf Tobias](/wiki/Rudolf_Tobias "Rudolf Tobias"), [Alexander Borodin](/wiki/Alexander_Borodin "Alexander Borodin"), [Mikhail Glinka](/wiki/Mikhail_Glinka "Mikhail Glinka"), [Modest Mussorgsky](/wiki/Modest_Mussorgsky "Modest Mussorgsky") and [Dmitri Shostakovich](/wiki/Dmitri_Shostakovich "Dmitri Shostakovich"), who taught at the conservatory during the 1960s, bringing it additional fame. The renowned Russian composer [Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov](/wiki/Nikolai_Rimsky-Korsakov "Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov") also taught at the conservatory from 1871 to 1905. Among his students were [Igor Stravinsky](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky"), [Alexander Glazounov](/wiki/Alexander_Glazounov "Alexander Glazounov"), [Anatoly Liadov](/wiki/Anatoly_Liadov "Anatoly Liadov") and others. The former St. Petersburg apartment of Rimsky-Korsakov has been faithfully preserved as the [composer's only museum](/wiki/Rimsky-Korsakov_Apartment_and_Museum "Rimsky-Korsakov Apartment and Museum").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Shou_alie_parusa.jpg/250px-Shou_alie_parusa.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shou_alie_parusa.jpg)

[Scarlet Sails](/wiki/Scarlet_Sails_(tradition) "Scarlet Sails (tradition)") celebration on the [Neva River](/wiki/Neva_River "Neva River")

Dmitri Shostakovich, who was born and raised in Saint Petersburg, dedicated his [Seventh Symphony](/wiki/Symphony_No._7_(Shostakovich) "Symphony No. 7 (Shostakovich)") to the city, calling it the "Leningrad Symphony". He wrote the symphony while based in the city during the siege of Leningrad. It was premiered in Samara in March 1942; a few months later, it received its first [performance](/wiki/Leningrad_premi%C3%A8re_of_Shostakovich%27s_Symphony_No._7 "Leningrad première of Shostakovich's Symphony No. 7") in the besieged Leningrad at the Bolshoy Philharmonic Hall under the baton of conductor [Karl Eliasberg](/wiki/Karl_Eliasberg "Karl Eliasberg"). It was heard over the radio and was said to have lifted the spirits of the surviving population.[[130]](#cite_note-136) In 1992, the 7th Symphony was performed by the 14 surviving orchestral players of the Leningrad premiere in the same hall as half a century before.[[131]](#cite_note-137) The [Leningrad Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Leningrad_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Leningrad Philharmonic Orchestra") remained one of the best known [symphony orchestras](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra") in the world under the leadership of conductors [Yevgeny Mravinsky](/wiki/Yevgeny_Mravinsky "Yevgeny Mravinsky") and [Yuri Temirkanov](/wiki/Yuri_Temirkanov "Yuri Temirkanov"). Mravinsky's term as artistic director of the Leningrad Philharmonic – a term that is possibly the longest of any conductor with any orchestra in modern times – led the orchestra from a little-known provincial ensemble to one of the world's most highly regarded orchestras, especially for the performance of Russian music.

The Imperial Choral Capella was founded and modelled after the [royal courts](/wiki/Royal_court "Royal court") of other European capitals.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/%D0%90%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%81%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80_%28Alexandrinsky_Theatre%29_%D0%B2_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BA%D1%82-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3%D0%B5..._2H1A8042WI.jpg/250px-%D0%90%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%81%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80_%28Alexandrinsky_Theatre%29_%D0%B2_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BA%D1%82-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3%D0%B5..._2H1A8042WI.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%90%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%81%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D1%80%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80_(Alexandrinsky_Theatre)_%D0%B2_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BA%D1%82-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3%D0%B5..._2H1A8042WI.jpg)

The [Alexandrinsky Theatre](/wiki/Alexandrinsky_Theatre "Alexandrinsky Theatre")

Saint Petersburg has been home to the newest movements in [popular music](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") in the country. The early Soviet [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") bands founded here included [Leopold Teplitsky](/w/index.php?title=Leopold_Teplitsky&action=edit&redlink=1 "Leopold Teplitsky (page does not exist)")'s First Concert Jazz Band (1927), [Leonid Utyosov](/wiki/Leonid_Utyosov "Leonid Utyosov") 's TheaJazz (1928, under the patronage of composer [Isaak Dunayevsky](/wiki/Isaak_Dunayevsky "Isaak Dunayevsky")), and Georgy Landsberg's Jazz Cappella (1929). The first jazz appreciation society in the Soviet Union was founded here in 1958 as J58, and later named [jazz club](/wiki/Jazz_club "Jazz club") Kvadrat. In 1956, the popular ensemble Druzhba was founded by Aleksandr Bronevitsky and [Edita Piekha](/wiki/Edita_Piekha "Edita Piekha") to become the first popular band in the USSR during the 1950s. In the 1960s, student rock-groups Argonavty, Kochevniki, and others pioneered a series of unofficial and [underground rock](/wiki/Underground_rock "Underground rock") concerts and festivals. In 1972, [Boris Grebenshchikov](/wiki/Boris_Grebenshchikov "Boris Grebenshchikov") founded the band [Aquarium](/wiki/Aquarium_(band) "Aquarium (band)"), which later grew to huge popularity. Since then, "Peter's rock" [music style](/wiki/Music_style "Music style") was formed.

In the 1970s many bands came out from the "underground" scene and eventually founded the [Leningrad Rock Club](/wiki/Leningrad_Rock_Club "Leningrad Rock Club"), which provided a stage to bands such as [DDT](/wiki/DDT_(band) "DDT (band)"), [Kino](/wiki/Kino_(band) "Kino (band)"), [Alisa](/wiki/Alisa_(Russian_band) "Alisa (Russian band)"), [Zemlyane](/wiki/Zemlyane "Zemlyane"), [Zoopark](/wiki/Zoopark_(band) "Zoopark (band)"), [Piknik](/wiki/Piknik "Piknik"), and [Secret](/wiki/Sekret_(band) "Sekret (band)"). The first Russian-style happening show [Pop Mekhanika](/wiki/Pop-Mechanics "Pop-Mechanics"), mixing over 300 people and animals on stage, was directed by the multi-talented [Sergey Kuryokhin](/wiki/Sergey_Kuryokhin "Sergey Kuryokhin") in the 1980s. The Sergey Kuryokhin International Festival (SKIF) is named after him. In 2004, the [Kuryokhin Center](/wiki/Kuryokhin_Center "Kuryokhin Center") was founded, where the SKIF and the Electro-Mechanica and Ethnomechanica festivals take place. SKIF focuses on [experimental pop music](/wiki/Experimental_pop_music "Experimental pop music") and [avant-garde music](/wiki/Avant-garde_music "Avant-garde music"), Electro-Mechanica on [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music"), and Ethnomechanica on [world music](/wiki/World_music "World music").

Today's Saint Petersburg boasts many notable musicians of various genres, from popular Leningrad's [Sergei Shnurov](/wiki/Sergei_Shnurov "Sergei Shnurov"), [Tequilajazzz](/wiki/Tequilajazzz "Tequilajazzz"), [Splean](/wiki/Splean "Splean"), and [Korol i Shut](/wiki/Korol_i_Shut "Korol i Shut"), to rock veterans [Yuri Shevchuk](/wiki/Yuri_Shevchuk "Yuri Shevchuk"), [Vyacheslav Butusov](/wiki/Vyacheslav_Butusov "Vyacheslav Butusov"), and [Mikhail Boyarsky](/wiki/Mikhail_Boyarsky "Mikhail Boyarsky"). In the early 2000s the city saw a wave of popularity of [metalcore](/wiki/Metalcore "Metalcore"), [rapcore](/wiki/Rapcore "Rapcore"), and [emocore](/wiki/Emocore "Emocore"), and there are bands such as [Amatory](/wiki/Amatory "Amatory"), [Kirpichi](/wiki/Kirpichi "Kirpichi"), Psychea, [Stigmata](/wiki/Stigmata_(Russian_band) "Stigmata (Russian band)"), [Grenouer](/wiki/Grenouer "Grenouer") and [Animal Jazz](/wiki/Animal_Jazz "Animal Jazz").

The [White Nights Festival](/wiki/White_Nights_Festival "White Nights Festival") in Saint Petersburg is famous for spectacular fireworks and a massive show celebrating the end of the [school year](/wiki/Academic_term "Academic term").

The rave band [Little Big](/wiki/Little_Big "Little Big") also hails from Saint Petersburg. Their music video for "[Skibidi](/wiki/Skibidi_(song) "Skibidi (song)")" was filmed in the city, starting at Akademicheskiy Pereulok.[[132]](#cite_note-138)

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Literature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Spb_Vasilievsky_Island_Pushkin_House_asv2019-09_img10.jpg/250px-Spb_Vasilievsky_Island_Pushkin_House_asv2019-09_img10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spb_Vasilievsky_Island_Pushkin_House_asv2019-09_img10.jpg)

The [Pushkin House](/wiki/Pushkin_House "Pushkin House")

Saint Petersburg has a longstanding and world-famous tradition in literature. [Dostoyevsky](/wiki/Dostoyevsky "Dostoyevsky") called it "The most abstract and intentional city in the world", emphasizing its artificiality, but it was also a symbol of modern disorder in a changing Russia. It often appeared to [Russian writers](/wiki/Russian_writers "Russian writers") as a menacing and inhuman mechanism. The grotesque and often nightmarish image of the city is featured in Pushkin's last poems, the Petersburg stories of [Gogol](/wiki/Gogol "Gogol"), the novels of [Dostoyevsky](/wiki/Dostoyevsky "Dostoyevsky"), the verse of [Alexander Blok](/wiki/Alexander_Blok "Alexander Blok") and [Osip Mandelshtam](/wiki/Osip_Mandelshtam "Osip Mandelshtam"), and in the symbolist novel *[Petersburg](/wiki/Petersburg_(novel) "Petersburg (novel)")* by [Andrey Bely](/wiki/Andrey_Bely "Andrey Bely"). According to Lotman in his chapter, 'The Symbolism of Saint Petersburg' in *Universe and the Mind*, these writers were inspired by symbolism from within the city itself. The effect of life in Saint Petersburg on the plight of the poor clerk in a society obsessed with hierarchy and status also became an important theme for authors such as [Pushkin](/wiki/Pushkin "Pushkin"), Gogol, and Dostoyevsky. Another important feature of early Saint Petersburg literature is its mythical element, which incorporates [urban legends](/wiki/Urban_legend "Urban legend") and popular [ghost stories](/wiki/Ghost_stories "Ghost stories"), as the stories of Pushkin and Gogol included ghosts returning to Saint Petersburg to haunt other characters as well as other fantastical elements, creating a surreal and abstract image of Saint Petersburg.

Twentieth-century writers from Saint Petersburg, such as [Vladimir Nabokov](/wiki/Vladimir_Nabokov "Vladimir Nabokov"), [Ayn Rand](/wiki/Ayn_Rand "Ayn Rand"), Andrey Bely and [Yevgeny Zamyatin](/wiki/Yevgeny_Zamyatin "Yevgeny Zamyatin"), along with his apprentices, The [Serapion Brothers](/wiki/Serapion_Brothers "Serapion Brothers"), created entirely new styles in literature and contributed new insights to the understanding of society through their experience in this city. [Anna Akhmatova](/wiki/Anna_Akhmatova "Anna Akhmatova") became an important leader for [Russian poetry](/wiki/Russian_poetry "Russian poetry"). Her poem *Requiem* adumbrates the perils encountered during the Stalinist era. Another notable 20th-century writer from Saint Petersburg is [Joseph Brodsky](/wiki/Joseph_Brodsky "Joseph Brodsky"), recipient of the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") (1987). While living in the United States, his writings in English reflected on life in Saint Petersburg from the unique perspective of being both an insider and an outsider to the city in essays such as "A Guide to a Renamed City" and the nostalgic "In a Room and a Half".[[133]](#cite_note-139)

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Film")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Konstantin_Khabensky_%E2%80%94_2018_%283%29.jpg/250px-Konstantin_Khabensky_%E2%80%94_2018_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Konstantin_Khabensky_%E2%80%94_2018_(3).jpg)

[Konstantin Khabensky](/wiki/Konstantin_Khabensky "Konstantin Khabensky"), known for his roles in *[Night Watch](/wiki/Night_Watch_(2004_film) "Night Watch (2004 film)")*, *[Day Watch](/wiki/Day_Watch_(film) "Day Watch (film)")* and *[Admiral](/wiki/The_Admiral_(2008_film) "The Admiral (2008 film)")*, is a native of Saint Petersburg.

Over 300 international and Russian movies were filmed in Saint Petersburg.[[134]](#cite_note-140) Well over a thousand feature films about tsars, revolution, people and stories set in Saint Petersburg have been produced worldwide but not filmed in the city. The first [film studios](/wiki/Film_studios "Film studios") were founded in Saint Petersburg in the 20th century, and since the 1920s, [Lenfilm](/wiki/Lenfilm "Lenfilm") has been the largest film studio based in Saint Petersburg. The first foreign feature movie filmed entirely in Saint Petersburg was the 1997 production of Tolstoy's *[Anna Karenina](/wiki/Anna_Karenina_(1997_film) "Anna Karenina (1997 film)")*, starring [Sophie Marceau](/wiki/Sophie_Marceau "Sophie Marceau") and [Sean Bean](/wiki/Sean_Bean "Sean Bean") and made by an international team of British, American, French, and Russian filmmakers.

The cult comedy *[Irony of Fate](/wiki/Irony_of_Fate "Irony of Fate")*[[135]](#cite_note-141) (also Ирония судьбы, или С лёгким паром!) is set in Saint Petersburg and pokes fun at Soviet city planning. The 1985 film *[White Nights](/wiki/White_Nights_(1985_film) "White Nights (1985 film)")* received considerable Western attention for having captured genuine Leningrad street scenes at a time when filming in the Soviet Union by Western production companies was generally unheard of. Other movies include *[GoldenEye](/wiki/GoldenEye "GoldenEye")* (1995), *[Midnight in Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Midnight_in_Saint_Petersburg "Midnight in Saint Petersburg")* (1996), *[Brother](/wiki/Brother_(1997_film) "Brother (1997 film)")* (1997) and [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_cinema "Tamil cinema") romantic [thriller film](/wiki/Thriller_(genre) "Thriller (genre)")-*[Dhaam Dhoom](/wiki/Dhaam_Dhoom "Dhaam Dhoom")* (2008). *[Onegin](/wiki/Eugene_Onegin#Film "Eugene Onegin")* (1999) is based on the [Pushkin](/wiki/Pushkin "Pushkin") poem and showcases many [tourist attractions](/wiki/Tourist_attraction "Tourist attraction"). In addition, the Russian romantic comedy, *[Piter FM](/wiki/Piter_FM "Piter FM")*, intricately showcases the cityscape, almost as if it were a main character in the film.

Several international film festivals are held annually, such as the [Festival of Festivals](/wiki/Festival_of_Festivals,_Saint_Petersburg "Festival of Festivals, Saint Petersburg"), Saint Petersburg, as well as the Message to Man International Documentary Film Festival, since its inauguration in 1988 during the White Nights.[[136]](#cite_note-Message_To_Man-142)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [List of higher education and academic institutions in Saint Petersburg](/wiki/List_of_higher_education_and_academic_institutions_in_Saint_Petersburg "List of higher education and academic institutions in Saint Petersburg")

As of 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit)–2007, there were 1,024 kindergartens, 716 [public schools](/wiki/Public_school_(government_funded) "Public school (government funded)") and 80 [vocational](/wiki/Vocational_education "Vocational education") schools in Saint Petersburg.[[138]](#cite_note-144) The largest of the public higher education institutions is [Saint Petersburg State University](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_State_University "Saint Petersburg State University"), enrolling approximately 32,000 undergraduate students;[[139]](#cite_note-145) and the largest non-governmental higher education institutions is the [Institute of International Economic Relations, Economics, and Law](/wiki/St._Petersburg_Institute_of_International_Trade,_Economics_and_Law "St. Petersburg Institute of International Trade, Economics and Law"). Other famous universities are [Saint Petersburg Polytechnic University](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Polytechnic_University "Saint Petersburg Polytechnic University"), [Herzen University](/wiki/Herzen_University "Herzen University"), [Saint Petersburg State University of Economics and Finance](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_State_University_of_Economics_and_Finance "Saint Petersburg State University of Economics and Finance"), [Saint Petersburg State University of Architecture and Civil Engineering](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_State_University_of_Architecture_and_Civil_Engineering "Saint Petersburg State University of Architecture and Civil Engineering"), and [Saint Petersburg Military engineering-technical university](/wiki/Military_engineering-technical_university "Military engineering-technical university"). However, the public universities are all federal property and do not belong to the city.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/SPB-Univer-12kol.jpg/960px-SPB-Univer-12kol.jpg)](/wiki/File:SPB-Univer-12kol.jpg "The Twelve Collegia of Saint Petersburg State University")

The [Twelve Collegia](/wiki/Twelve_Collegia "Twelve Collegia") of [Saint Petersburg State University](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_State_University "Saint Petersburg State University")

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sport in Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Sport_in_Saint_Petersburg "Sport in Saint Petersburg")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Saint_Petersburg%2C_Russia_%2843506287930%29.jpg/250px-Saint_Petersburg%2C_Russia_%2843506287930%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saint_Petersburg,_Russia_(43506287930).jpg)

[Gazprom Arena](/wiki/Krestovsky_Stadium "Krestovsky Stadium") on [Krestovsky Island](/wiki/Krestovsky_Island "Krestovsky Island")

Leningrad hosted part of the [association football tournament](/wiki/Football_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1980 Summer Olympics") during the 1980 [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics"). The 1994 [Goodwill Games](/wiki/Goodwill_Games "Goodwill Games") were also held here.[[140]](#cite_note-146)

In boating, the first competition here was the 1703 [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)") event initiated by Peter the Great, after the victory over the [Swedish fleet](/wiki/Swedish_Navy "Swedish Navy"). The Russian Navy has held [Yachting](/wiki/Yachting "Yachting") events since the foundation of the city. [Yacht clubs](/wiki/Yacht_club "Yacht club"):[[141]](#cite_note-147) [St. Petersburg River Yacht Club](/wiki/St._Petersburg_River_Yacht_Club "St. Petersburg River Yacht Club"), [Neva Yacht Club](/wiki/Neva_Yacht_Club "Neva Yacht Club"), the latter is the oldest yacht club in the world. In the winter, when the sea and lake surfaces are frozen and yachts and dinghies cannot be used, local people sail [ice boats](/wiki/Ice_boat "Ice boat").

[Equestrianism](/wiki/Equestrianism "Equestrianism") has been a long tradition, popular among the Tsars and the aristocracy, as well as part of [military training](/wiki/Military_training "Military training"). Several historic sports arenas were built for equestrianism since the 18th century to maintain training all year round, such as the Zimny Stadion and Konnogvardeisky Manezh.

[Chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess") tradition was highlighted by the 1914 international tournament, partially funded by the Tsar, in which the title "Grandmaster" was first formally conferred by [Russian Tsar Nicholas II](/wiki/Russian_Tsar_Nicholas_II "Russian Tsar Nicholas II") to five players: [Lasker](/wiki/Emanuel_Lasker "Emanuel Lasker"), [Capablanca](/wiki/Capablanca "Capablanca"), [Alekhine](/wiki/Alekhine "Alekhine"), [Tarrasch](/wiki/Siegbert_Tarrasch "Siegbert Tarrasch") and [Marshall](/wiki/Frank_Marshall_(chess_player) "Frank Marshall (chess player)").

The city's main football team is [FC Zenit Saint Petersburg](/wiki/FC_Zenit_Saint_Petersburg "FC Zenit Saint Petersburg"), who have been champions of the [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Top_League "Soviet Top League") and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Premier_League "Russian Premier League") league ten times, most notably claiming the RPL title in six consecutive seasons from [2018–19](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Russian_Premier_League "2018–19 Russian Premier League") to [2024–25](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Russian_Premier_League "2024–25 Russian Premier League"), along with winning the [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Cup "Soviet Cup")/[Russian Cup](/wiki/Russian_Cup_(football) "Russian Cup (football)") six times. The club also won the [2007–08 UEFA Cup](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_UEFA_Cup "2007–08 UEFA Cup") and the [2008 UEFA Super Cup](/wiki/2008_UEFA_Super_Cup "2008 UEFA Super Cup"), spearheaded by successful player and local hero [Andrey Arshavin](/wiki/Andrey_Arshavin "Andrey Arshavin").

[Kirov Stadium](/wiki/Kirov_Stadium "Kirov Stadium") formerly existed as Zenit's home from 1950 to 1993 and again in 1995, being one of the largest stadiums in the world at the time. In 1951, a crowd of 110,000 set the single-game attendance record for Soviet football. The stadium was knocked down in 2006, with Zenit temporarily moving to the [Petrovsky Stadium](/wiki/Petrovsky_Stadium "Petrovsky Stadium") before the [Krestovsky Stadium](/wiki/Krestovsky_Stadium "Krestovsky Stadium") was built on the same site as the Kirov Stadium. The Krestovsky Stadium opened in 2017, hosting four matches at the [2017 FIFA Confederations Cup](/wiki/2017_FIFA_Confederations_Cup "2017 FIFA Confederations Cup"), including the [final](/wiki/2017_FIFA_Confederations_Cup_Final "2017 FIFA Confederations Cup Final"). The stadium then hosted seven matches at the [2018 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup "2018 FIFA World Cup"), including a semi-final and the third-placed playoff. It also hosted seven matches at [UEFA Euro 2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020"), including a quarter-final. The stadium was going to host the [2022 UEFA Champions League final](/wiki/2022_UEFA_Champions_League_final "2022 UEFA Champions League final"), however UEFA removed Saint Petersburg as host in February 2022, citing the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine").[[142]](#cite_note-148)

Hockey teams in the city include [SKA Saint Petersburg](/wiki/SKA_Saint_Petersburg "SKA Saint Petersburg") in the [KHL](/wiki/KHL "KHL"), [SKA-VMF](/wiki/SKA-VMF "SKA-VMF") and [Dinamo Saint Petersburg](/wiki/HC_Dinamo_Saint_Petersburg "HC Dinamo Saint Petersburg") in the [All-Russian Hockey League](/wiki/All-Russian_Hockey_League "All-Russian Hockey League"), and junior clubs [SKA-1946](/wiki/SKA-1946 "SKA-1946") and [SKA Academy](/wiki/SKA_Varyagi "SKA Varyagi") in the [Youth Hockey League](/wiki/Youth_Hockey_League "Youth Hockey League"). In addition, the [Shanghai Dragons](/wiki/Shanghai_Dragons_(ice_hockey) "Shanghai Dragons (ice hockey)") hockey club is temporarily located in the city as of the [2025-26 KHL season](/wiki/2025-26_KHL_season "2025-26 KHL season"). SKA Saint Petersburg is one of the most popular in the KHL, consistently being at or near the top of the league in attendance. Along with their popularity, they are one of the best teams in the KHL right now, as they have won the [Gagarin Cup](/wiki/Gagarin_Cup "Gagarin Cup") twice.[[143]](#cite_note-149) Well-known players on the team include [Pavel Datsyuk](/wiki/Pavel_Datsyuk "Pavel Datsyuk"), [Ilya Kovalchuk](/wiki/Ilya_Kovalchuk "Ilya Kovalchuk"), [Nikita Gusev](/wiki/Nikita_Gusev "Nikita Gusev"), [Sergei Shirokov](/wiki/Sergei_Shirokov "Sergei Shirokov") and [Viktor Tikhonov](/wiki/Viktor_Tikhonov_(ice_hockey_b._1988) "Viktor Tikhonov (ice hockey b. 1988)"). During the NHL lockout, stars Ilya Kovalchuk, [Sergei Bobrovsky](/wiki/Sergei_Bobrovsky "Sergei Bobrovsky"), and [Vladimir Tarasenko](/wiki/Vladimir_Tarasenko "Vladimir Tarasenko") also played for the team. They play their home games at [SKA Arena](/wiki/SKA_Arena "SKA Arena").

The city's long-time basketball team is [BC Spartak Saint Petersburg](/wiki/BC_Spartak_Saint_Petersburg "BC Spartak Saint Petersburg"), which launched the career of [Andrei Kirilenko](/wiki/Andrei_Kirilenko "Andrei Kirilenko"). BC Spartak Saint Petersburg won two championships in the [USSR Premier League](/wiki/USSR_Premier_Basketball_League "USSR Premier Basketball League") (1975 and 1992), two [USSR Cups](/wiki/USSR_Basketball_Cup "USSR Basketball Cup") (1978 and 1987), and a [Russian Cup](/wiki/Russian_Basketball_Cup "Russian Basketball Cup") title (2011). They also won the [Saporta Cup](/wiki/Saporta_Cup "Saporta Cup") twice (1973 and 1975). Legends of the club include [Alexander Belov](/wiki/Alexander_Belov "Alexander Belov") and [Vladimir Kondrashin](/wiki/Vladimir_Kondrashin "Vladimir Kondrashin"). [BC Zenit Saint Petersburg](/wiki/BC_Zenit_Saint_Petersburg "BC Zenit Saint Petersburg") also plays in the city, being formed in 2014.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Spb_06-2017_img46_ZSD_bridge_at_Krestovsky_Stadium.jpg/250px-Spb_06-2017_img46_ZSD_bridge_at_Krestovsky_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spb_06-2017_img46_ZSD_bridge_at_Krestovsky_Stadium.jpg)

A section of the [Western High-Speed Diameter](/wiki/Western_High-Speed_Diameter "Western High-Speed Diameter")

Saint Petersburg is a major transport hub. The first Russian railway was built here in 1837, and since then, the city's transport infrastructure has kept pace with the city's growth. Petersburg has an extensive system of local roads and railway services, maintains a large public transport system that includes the [Saint Petersburg tram](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_tram "Saint Petersburg tram") and the [Saint Petersburg Metro](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Metro "Saint Petersburg Metro"), and is home to several riverine services that convey passengers around the city efficiently and in relative comfort.

The city is connected to the rest of Russia and the wider world by several federal highways and national and international rail routes. [Pulkovo Airport](/wiki/Pulkovo_Airport "Pulkovo Airport") serves most of the air passengers departing from or arriving in the city.

### Public transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Public transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Tram_71-923_0201_on_route_100.jpg/250px-Tram_71-923_0201_on_route_100.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tram_71-923_0201_on_route_100.jpg)

Tram “Bogatyr” on route №100

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Metro_SPB_Line1_Narvskaya1.jpg/250px-Metro_SPB_Line1_Narvskaya1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Metro_SPB_Line1_Narvskaya1.jpg)

[Narvskaya](/wiki/Narvskaya "Narvskaya") station of the [Saint Petersburg Metro](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Metro "Saint Petersburg Metro"), opened in 1955

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/AKSM-32100D_in_SPB_%28img2%29.jpg/250px-AKSM-32100D_in_SPB_%28img2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:AKSM-32100D_in_SPB_(img2).jpg)

[Trolleybus](/wiki/Trolleybus "Trolleybus") on [Nevsky Prospekt](/wiki/Nevsky_Prospekt "Nevsky Prospekt")

Saint Petersburg has an extensive city-funded network of public transport including [buses](/wiki/Buses_in_Saint_Petersburg "Buses in Saint Petersburg"), [trams](/wiki/Trams_in_Saint_Petersburg "Trams in Saint Petersburg"), and [trolleybuses](/w/index.php?title=Trolleybuses_in_St._Petersburg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Trolleybuses in St. Petersburg (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BA%D1%82-%D0%BF%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%82%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%BB%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%B9%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%81 "ru:Санкт-петербургский троллейбус")].

In the 1980s, the city had the largest tram network globally.[[144]](#cite_note-150)[[145]](#cite_note-151) However, like in many Russian cities, trams were dismantled after the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union").[[146]](#cite_note-152)

Buses carry up to 3 million passengers daily, with over 250 urban and suburban bus routes.

[Saint Petersburg Metro](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Metro "Saint Petersburg Metro") was opened in 1955; it now has 6 lines with 75 stations, connecting all five railway terminals,[[147]](#cite_note-153) and carrying 2.3 million passengers daily. Many stations are elaborately decorated with materials such as marble and bronze. There are plans for extending several lines and building one new depot.[[148]](#cite_note-154) Plans call for 16 new stations to open between 2025 and 2035, including 10 that will open between 2025 and 2030.[[149]](#cite_note-155) The Admiralteysko-Okhtinskaya and Koltsevaya line is projected to open after the 2030s.[[150]](#cite_note-156)
