### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Roads")]

[Traffic jams](/wiki/Traffic_jams "Traffic jams") are common in the city due to daily commuter traffic volumes, intercity traffic, and excessive winter snow. The construction of [freeways](/wiki/Freeways "Freeways") such as the [Saint Petersburg Ring Road](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg_Ring_Road "Saint Petersburg Ring Road"), completed in 2011, and the [Western High-Speed Diameter](/wiki/Western_High-Speed_Diameter "Western High-Speed Diameter"), completed in 2017, helped reduce traffic in the city. The [M11 Neva](/wiki/Moscow%E2%80%93Saint_Petersburg_motorway "Moscow–Saint Petersburg motorway"), also known as the Moscow-Saint Petersburg Motorway, is a [federal highway](/wiki/Russian_federal_highway "Russian federal highway"), and connects Saint Petersburg to Moscow by a freeway.

Saint Petersburg is an important [transport corridor](/wiki/Transport_corridor "Transport corridor") linking [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") to Russia and Eastern Europe. The city is a node of the [international European routes](/wiki/International_E-road_network "International E-road network") [E18](/wiki/European_route_E18 "European route E18") towards [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), [E20](/wiki/European_route_E20 "European route E20") towards [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn"), [E95](/wiki/European_route_E95 "European route E95") towards [Pskov](/wiki/Pskov "Pskov"), [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv") and [Odesa](/wiki/Odesa "Odesa") and [E105](/wiki/European_route_E105 "European route E105") towards [Petrozavodsk](/wiki/Petrozavodsk "Petrozavodsk"), [Murmansk](/wiki/Murmansk "Murmansk") and [Kirkenes](/wiki/Kirkenes "Kirkenes") (north) and towards Moscow and [Kharkiv](/wiki/Kharkiv "Kharkiv") (south).

### Waterways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Waterways")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/St._Petersburg_Russia_Hydrofoil_boat.jpg/250px-St._Petersburg_Russia_Hydrofoil_boat.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Petersburg_Russia_Hydrofoil_boat.jpg)

[Meteor hydrofoil](/wiki/Meteor_(hydrofoil) "Meteor (hydrofoil)") docking in Saint Petersburg upon arrival from [Peterhof Palace](/wiki/Peterhof_Palace "Peterhof Palace") (2008)

The city is also served by passenger and cargo seaports[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] in the [Neva Bay](/wiki/Neva_Bay "Neva Bay") of the [Gulf of Finland](/wiki/Gulf_of_Finland "Gulf of Finland"), Baltic Sea, the river port higher up the Neva and tens of smaller passenger stations on both banks of the Neva river. It is a terminus of both the [Volga–Baltic](/wiki/Volga%E2%80%93Baltic_Waterway "Volga–Baltic Waterway") and [White Sea–Baltic](/wiki/White_Sea%E2%80%93Baltic_Canal "White Sea–Baltic Canal") waterways.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The first high bridge that does not need to be drawn, the 2,824-metre-long (9,265 ft) [Big Obukhovsky Bridge](/wiki/Big_Obukhovsky_Bridge "Big Obukhovsky Bridge") opened in 2004. [Meteor hydrofoils](/wiki/Meteor_(hydrofoil) "Meteor (hydrofoil)") link the city centre to the coastal towns of [Kronstadt](/wiki/Kronstadt "Kronstadt") and [Shlisselburg](/wiki/Shlisselburg "Shlisselburg") from May through October.[[151]](#cite_note-157) In the warmer months, many smaller boats and water-taxis navigate the city's canals.

The shipping company [St. Peter Line](/wiki/St._Peter_Line "St. Peter Line") operates two ferries that sail from [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki") to Saint Petersburg and from [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm") to Saint Petersburg.[[152]](#cite_note-158)

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Rail")]

See also: [Rail transport in Russia](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Russia "Rail transport in Russia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BF%D1%81%D0%B0%D0%BD_%D0%B2_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BA%D1%82-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3.jpg/250px-%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BF%D1%81%D0%B0%D0%BD_%D0%B2_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BA%D1%82-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BF%D1%81%D0%B0%D0%BD_%D0%B2_%D0%A1%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BA%D1%82-%D0%9F%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B1%D1%83%D1%80%D0%B3.jpg)

The [Sapsan](/wiki/Sapsan "Sapsan") high-speed train runs between Saint Petersburg and Moscow.

The city is the final destination for a web of intercity and suburban railways, served by five different railway terminals ([Baltiysky](/wiki/Baltiysky_Rail_Terminal "Baltiysky Rail Terminal"), [Finlyandsky](/wiki/Finlyandsky_Rail_Terminal "Finlyandsky Rail Terminal"), [Ladozhsky](/wiki/Ladozhsky_Rail_Terminal "Ladozhsky Rail Terminal"), [Moskovsky](/wiki/Moskovsky_Rail_Terminal_(Saint_Petersburg) "Moskovsky Rail Terminal (Saint Petersburg)") and [Vitebsky](/wiki/Vitebsky_Rail_Terminal "Vitebsky Rail Terminal")),[[g]](#cite_note-159)[[153]](#cite_note-160) as well as dozens of non-terminal [railway stations](/wiki/Railway_stations "Railway stations") within the federal subject. Saint Petersburg has international railway connections to [Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), Finland; Berlin, Germany; and many former republics of the USSR. The [Helsinki railway](/wiki/Riihim%C3%A4ki_%E2%80%93_Saint_Petersburg_Railway "Riihimäki – Saint Petersburg Railway"), built in 1870 and 443 kilometres (275 mi) long, had until 2022 trains running five times a day, in a journey lasting about three and a half hours with the [Allegro](/wiki/Karelian_Trains_Class_Sm6 "Karelian Trains Class Sm6") train.

The [Moscow–Saint Petersburg Railway](/wiki/Moscow%E2%80%93Saint_Petersburg_Railway "Moscow–Saint Petersburg Railway") opened in 1851, and is 651 kilometres (405 mi) long; the commute to Moscow now requires from three and a half to nine hours.[[154]](#cite_note-161)

In 2009, [Russian Railways](/wiki/Russian_Railways "Russian Railways") launched a high-speed service for the Moscow–Saint Petersburg route. The new train, known as [Sapsan](/wiki/Sapsan "Sapsan"), is a derivative of the popular [Siemens Velaro](/wiki/Siemens_Velaro "Siemens Velaro") train; various versions of this already operate in some European countries. It set records for the fastest train in Russia on 2 May 2009, travelling at 281 kilometres per hour (175 mph)[[155]](#cite_note-162) and on 7 May 2009, travelling at 290 kilometres per hour (180 mph).

From 12 December 2010 until March 2022, [Karelian Trains](/wiki/Karelian_Trains "Karelian Trains"), a joint venture between [Russian Railways](/wiki/Russian_Railways "Russian Railways") and [VR (Finnish Railways)](/wiki/VR_Group "VR Group"), has been running [Alstom Pendolino](/wiki/Karelian_Trains_Class_Sm6 "Karelian Trains Class Sm6") operated high-speed services between Saint Petersburg's [Finlyandsky](/wiki/Finlyandsky_Rail_Terminal "Finlyandsky Rail Terminal") and Helsinki's [Central](/wiki/Helsinki_Central_railway_station "Helsinki Central railway station") railway stations. These services are branded as "Allegro" trains. "Allegro" is known for suffering from some big technical problems from time to time, which sometimes result in significant delays and even cancellation of tourists' trips.[[156]](#cite_note-163) The service has been suspended indefinitely in the context of the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine") and is not expected to resume.

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Air")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/%D0%9F%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%8F.jpg/500px-%D0%9F%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%8F.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9F%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B0_%D0%BD%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B0%D1%8F.jpg)

Pulkovo International Airport

Saint Petersburg is served by [Pulkovo International Airport](/wiki/Pulkovo_International_Airport "Pulkovo International Airport").[[157]](#cite_note-164)

Pulkovo airport was opened to passengers as a small aerodrome in 1931. As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit), the Pulkovo airport, which handles over 12 million passengers annually, is the 3rd busiest in Russia after Moscow's [Sheremetyevo](/wiki/Sheremetyevo "Sheremetyevo") and [Domodedovo](/wiki/Domodedovo_International_Airport "Domodedovo International Airport"). As a result, the steadily increasing passenger traffic has triggered a massive modernization of the entire airport infrastructure. A newly built Terminal 1 of the Pulkovo airport was put into operation on 4 December 2013 and integrated international flights of the former terminal Pulkovo-2. The renovated terminal Pulkovo-1 has been opened for domestic flights as an extension of Terminal 1 in 2015.[[158]](#cite_note-165) One of the oldest air carriers of the Russian Federation ([Rossiya Airlines](/wiki/Rossiya_Airlines "Rossiya Airlines")) is registered in Saint Petersburg and is the largest and the base carrier of Pulkovo Airport.[[159]](#cite_note-166)

There is a regular rapid-bus connection (buses 39, 39E, K39) between Pulkovo airport and the [Moskovskaya metro station](/wiki/Moskovskaya_(Saint_Petersburg_Metro) "Moskovskaya (Saint Petersburg Metro)") as well as a 24/7 taxi service.

### Former twin towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Former twin towns")]

Italian cities [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") and [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice") were formerly twin cities of Saint Petersburg, but suspended this link due to St. Petersburg's ban on "gay propaganda".[[208]](#cite_note-215) Milan suspended the relationship with Saint Petersburg on 23 November 2012[[209]](#cite_note-216) and Venice did so on 28 January 2013.[[210]](#cite_note-217)

Shortly after the beginning of the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk"), [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw "Warsaw"), [Aarhus](/wiki/Aarhus "Aarhus"), [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"), [Kotka](/wiki/Kotka "Kotka"), [Turku](/wiki/Turku "Turku"), [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga") and [Tallinn](/wiki/Tallinn "Tallinn") terminated or suspended the cooperation, affiliation or sister city relationship with Saint Petersburg.[[211]](#cite_note-218)[[212]](#cite_note-219)[[213]](#cite_note-220)[[214]](#cite_note-221)[[215]](#cite_note-222)[[216]](#cite_note-223)[[217]](#cite_note-224)[[218]](#cite_note-225)[[219]](#cite_note-226)[[220]](#cite_note-227) On 17 March 2022, [Košice](/wiki/Ko%C5%A1ice "Košice") joined the list of cities terminating the partnership. The cooperation began in 1995.[[221]](#cite_note-228)

### Twinning with occupied Mariupol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Petersburg&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Twinning with occupied Mariupol")]

Some Russian cities are twinned with ones in occupied Ukraine, in particular, Saint Petersburg is twinned with [Mariupol](/wiki/Mariupol "Mariupol").[[222]](#cite_note-229) An art symbol of the twinning was unveiled on [Palace Square](/wiki/Palace_Square "Palace Square") in Saint Petersburg, defaced and removed.[[223]](#cite_note-230)
